Answer.ValidTrueComment:
XCP-ng provides automated account management through XAPI Role-Based Access Control (RBAC).
The automated check verified: (1) RBAC roles are available via xe role-list (pool-admin, pool-operator, vm-power-admin, vm-admin, vm-operator, read-only), (2) RBAC subjects (users/groups) can be listed via xe subject-list showing external authentication integration, (3) Non-admin roles can be assigned to segregate privileges.
XAPI RBAC provides automated mechanisms for creating, modifying, and managing user accounts and access permissions on the hypervisor. This meets the VMM SRG requirement for automated account management functions.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment:
XCP-ng RBAC is not properly configured to support automated account management. The automated check found issues with RBAC role availability or subject configuration. Without properly configured RBAC, account management relies on manual root SSH access without role segregation.
Remediation: 1. Enable external authentication: xe pool-enable-external-auth auth-type=AD service-name=domain.com 2. Add RBAC subjects: xe subject-add subject-name=user role-name=vm-operator 3. Verify RBAC roles: xe role-list 4. Verify subjects: xe subject-list 5. Assign least-privilege roles per DoD policy
Contact the system administrator and ISSO to configure XAPI RBAC.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check verified that XCP-ng hypervisor temporary account policies enforce 72-hour expiration. The check examined local account expiration settings via chage, the INACTIVE parameter in /etc/default/useradd, and Dom0 account management policies. Temporary accounts are configured with appropriate expiration dates, and the organizational policy requires that temporary accounts are automatically disabled after 72 hours. XCP-ng Dom0, based on CentOS 7, uses the shadow password mechanism to enforce account expiration through the chage utility. The system administrator has configured temporary accounts with explicit expiration dates set no more than 72 hours from creation. This meets the VMM SRG requirement to automatically remove or disable temporary accounts after 72 hours.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check found that temporary account expiration policies are not properly configured on the XCP-ng hypervisor. The VMM SRG requires automatic removal or disabling of temporary accounts after 72 hours. To remediate: (1) When creating temporary accounts, always set an expiration date: useradd -e $(date -d '+72 hours' +%Y-%m-%d) tempuser. (2) For existing temporary accounts, set expiration: chage -E $(date -d '+72 hours' +%Y-%m-%d) tempuser. (3) Implement a cron job to audit and disable expired temporary accounts: create /etc/cron.daily/disable-temp-accounts. (4) Document the temporary account policy in the system security plan. The ISSO/ISSM must establish procedures for creating, tracking, and automatically disabling temporary accounts within the 72-hour window.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check verified that the XCP-ng hypervisor is configured to automatically disable accounts after 35 days of inactivity. The INACTIVE parameter in /etc/default/useradd is set to 35 or fewer days, and individual account inactivity settings were verified via chage. The shadow password mechanism automatically locks accounts when the password expiration date plus the inactivity period has passed without a login. This ensures that dormant accounts are disabled in a timely manner, reducing the attack surface from unused credentials. The organizational policy requires regular account reviews to identify and manage inactive accounts on hypervisor systems.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check found that account inactivity policies are not properly configured on the XCP-ng hypervisor. The VMM SRG requires automatic disabling of accounts after 35 days of inactivity. To remediate: (1) Set the default inactivity period: useradd -D -f 35. (2) For existing accounts: chage --inactive 35 username. (3) Verify: grep INACTIVE /etc/default/useradd (should show INACTIVE=35). (4) Audit all accounts: for u in $(awk -F: '{if ($3 >= 1000) print $1}' /etc/passwd); do chage -l $u; done. The system administrator must ensure all local accounts have the INACTIVE field set to 35 days or less, and the ISSO/ISSM must include account inactivity monitoring in the security management process.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check verified that the XCP-ng hypervisor audits account creation events. The auditd service is active and audit rules are configured to monitor changes to /etc/passwd, /etc/shadow, /etc/group, and /etc/gshadow, which capture all local account creation events. The audit rules use watch keys such as identity and audit_rules_usergroup_modification to tag account-related events. Additionally, xapi audit logs in /var/log/xensource.log record account and permission changes made through the XCP-ng management interface. This dual-layer auditing ensures comprehensive coverage of account creation activities on the hypervisor.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check found that account creation auditing is not properly configured on the XCP-ng hypervisor. The VMM SRG requires automatic auditing of account creation events. To remediate: (1) Ensure auditd is running: systemctl enable auditd && systemctl start auditd. (2) Add audit rules for account files in /etc/audit/rules.d/account.rules: -w /etc/passwd -p wa -k identity, -w /etc/shadow -p wa -k identity, -w /etc/group -p wa -k identity, -w /etc/gshadow -p wa -k identity. (3) Reload rules: augenrules --load. (4) Verify: auditctl -l | grep identity. The system administrator must ensure that all account creation methods (useradd, xapi, direct file editing) generate audit records.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment:
XCP-ng Dom0 enforces login attempt limits through PAM faillock configuration.
The automated check verified: (1) pam_faillock.so is configured in /etc/pam.d/system-auth and/or /etc/pam.d/password-auth, (2) deny parameter is set to 3 or fewer attempts, (3) fail_interval is set to 900 seconds (15 minutes) or more, (4) unlock_time is configured per organizational policy.
PAM faillock automatically locks accounts after the specified number of failed authentication attempts within the configured time window. This meets the VMM SRG requirement for limiting consecutive invalid logon attempts.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment:
XCP-ng Dom0 does not properly enforce login attempt limits. The automated check found that pam_faillock.so is not configured or parameters do not meet requirements.
Remediation: 1. Edit /etc/pam.d/system-auth and /etc/pam.d/password-auth to include: auth required pam_faillock.so preauth deny=3 fail_interval=900 unlock_time=never auth required pam_faillock.so authfail deny=3 fail_interval=900 unlock_time=never 2. Verify: faillock --user testuser 3. Test by attempting 4 failed logins and confirming lockout
Contact the system administrator to configure PAM faillock.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check verified that the XCP-ng hypervisor displays the Standard Mandatory DoD Notice and Consent Banner before granting access. The check examined /etc/issue for the SSH pre-authentication banner, /etc/motd for the post-login message, and the SSH Banner directive in /etc/ssh/sshd_config. The DoD consent banner text is present and configured to display before users can authenticate. The banner includes the required language regarding monitoring, recording, and consent to security testing. This meets the VMM SRG requirement to display the Standard Mandatory DoD Notice and Consent Banner prior to system access.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check found that the Standard Mandatory DoD Notice and Consent Banner is not properly configured on the XCP-ng hypervisor. The VMM SRG requires displaying the DoD consent banner before granting access. To remediate: (1) Create the banner in /etc/issue with the full DoD consent text. (2) Configure SSH to display it: set Banner /etc/issue in /etc/ssh/sshd_config. (3) Restart sshd: systemctl restart sshd. (4) For XenCenter/XO access, configure the login banner in the xapi management interface. The full banner text is available in the STIG and must include language about monitoring, recording, auditing, and consent to security testing. The ISSO/ISSM must verify the banner matches the exact DoD-required text.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check verified that the XCP-ng hypervisor retains the DoD consent banner until users explicitly acknowledge it and take action to log on. The SSH pre-authentication banner configured via /etc/issue is displayed before the password prompt, requiring the user to proceed with authentication (an explicit acknowledgment action) before gaining access. The banner remains visible until the user provides credentials. For console access, the /etc/issue file content is displayed before the login prompt. This configuration ensures that users must read and implicitly acknowledge the consent banner by proceeding with authentication, meeting the VMM SRG requirement for banner retention until explicit user action.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check found that the DoD consent banner retention mechanism is not properly configured on the XCP-ng hypervisor. The VMM SRG requires the banner to remain on screen until users explicitly acknowledge it. To remediate: (1) Ensure /etc/issue contains the full DoD consent banner. (2) Set Banner /etc/issue in /etc/ssh/sshd_config so it displays before authentication. (3) Restart sshd: systemctl restart sshd. (4) Verify by SSH-ing to the host and confirming the banner appears before the password prompt. (5) For XenCenter/XO web interface access, configure the management interface to display a banner dialog requiring explicit acknowledgment before login. The ISSO/ISSM must verify all access methods display and retain the banner appropriately.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check verified that the XCP-ng hypervisor limits concurrent sessions to ten or fewer per account. The check examined /etc/security/limits.conf and /etc/security/limits.d/ for maxlogins settings, SSH MaxSessions in /etc/ssh/sshd_config, and xapi session limits. The system is configured with appropriate session limits that restrict concurrent access to ten or fewer sessions per user. PAM limits enforcement via pam_limits.so ensures that the maxlogins parameter is applied to all login methods. This meets the VMM SRG requirement to limit concurrent sessions to ten for all account types.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check found that concurrent session limits are not properly configured on the XCP-ng hypervisor. The VMM SRG requires limiting concurrent sessions to ten per account. To remediate: (1) Set limits in /etc/security/limits.conf: * hard maxlogins 10. (2) Set SSH limits in /etc/ssh/sshd_config: MaxSessions 10. (3) Ensure pam_limits.so is in /etc/pam.d/system-auth and /etc/pam.d/password-auth. (4) Restart sshd: systemctl restart sshd. (5) Verify: ulimit -u and test with multiple concurrent SSH sessions. The system administrator must ensure all access methods (SSH, console, xapi) enforce the ten-session limit.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check verified that the XCP-ng hypervisor retains session locks until the user re-authenticates. The check examined SSH ClientAliveInterval and ClientAliveCountMax settings, tmux/screen lock configuration, and console lock mechanisms. When a session is locked (via timeout or manual action), the system requires the user to re-enter their password to unlock the session. SSH sessions that time out are terminated, requiring full re-authentication. The vlock or screen lock utility is available for console sessions. This meets the VMM SRG requirement that session locks persist until the user reestablishes access through proper authentication.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check found that session lock retention is not properly configured on the XCP-ng hypervisor. The VMM SRG requires session locks to persist until the user re-authenticates. To remediate: (1) Install vlock for console locking: yum install kbd (includes vlock). (2) Configure SSH session handling in /etc/ssh/sshd_config: ClientAliveInterval 900, ClientAliveCountMax 0 (terminates idle sessions, requiring re-authentication). (3) For tmux/screen sessions, configure lock commands that require password re-entry. (4) Verify that locked sessions cannot be resumed without authentication. The system administrator must ensure all access methods enforce re-authentication after session lock.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check verified that the XCP-ng hypervisor initiates session locks after 15 minutes of inactivity. The check examined SSH ClientAliveInterval (set to 900 seconds or less), TMOUT environment variable for shell sessions, and console timeout settings. SSH sessions are configured to detect inactivity within 15 minutes and either lock or terminate the session. The TMOUT variable is set in /etc/profile or /etc/profile.d/ to automatically close idle shell sessions. This meets the VMM SRG requirement to initiate a session lock after a 15-minute period of inactivity, preventing unauthorized access to unattended sessions.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check found that session inactivity timeout is not properly configured on the XCP-ng hypervisor. The VMM SRG requires a session lock after 15 minutes of inactivity. To remediate: (1) Set SSH timeout in /etc/ssh/sshd_config: ClientAliveInterval 900, ClientAliveCountMax 0. (2) Set shell timeout: echo 'TMOUT=900' > /etc/profile.d/tmout.sh and chmod 644 /etc/profile.d/tmout.sh. (3) Make TMOUT readonly: add 'readonly TMOUT' and 'export TMOUT' to the file. (4) Restart sshd: systemctl restart sshd. (5) Verify: ssh to the host and wait 15 minutes. The system administrator must ensure all shell types and access methods enforce the 15-minute inactivity timeout.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check verified that users can directly initiate a session lock on the XCP-ng hypervisor. The check confirmed availability of vlock for console session locking, tmux/screen lock capability for terminal multiplexers, and Ctrl+L or equivalent keyboard shortcuts. Users can manually lock their sessions at any time using the vlock command for console access or the lock-session command in tmux/screen. SSH sessions can be suspended and require re-authentication to resume. This meets the VMM SRG requirement to provide users with the capability to directly initiate a session lock without waiting for the automatic timeout.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check found that manual session lock capability is not properly available on the XCP-ng hypervisor. The VMM SRG requires that users can directly initiate session locks. To remediate: (1) Install vlock: yum install kbd (includes vlock utility). (2) Ensure tmux or screen is available: yum install tmux. (3) Configure tmux lock command: add 'bind L lock-session' to /etc/tmux.conf. (4) Create a lock alias for convenience: echo 'alias lock=vlock' >> /etc/profile.d/lock.sh. (5) Document the session lock procedure for users. The system administrator must ensure that at least one method for manual session locking is available for each access method (console, SSH, management interface).
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check verified that the XCP-ng hypervisor conceals previously visible information when a session is locked. The check examined vlock behavior (clears screen on lock), tmux/screen lock display settings, and SSH session termination behavior. When a session lock is initiated via vlock, the screen is cleared and a lock message is displayed, effectively concealing all previously visible information. SSH sessions that are terminated due to timeout also clear the terminal. This meets the VMM SRG requirement that session locks conceal previously visible information with a publicly viewable image or blank screen.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check found that session lock display concealment is not properly configured on the XCP-ng hypervisor. The VMM SRG requires that locked sessions conceal previously visible information. To remediate: (1) Install vlock: yum install kbd. When invoked, vlock clears the screen and displays only a lock message. (2) Configure tmux lock to clear the display: set lock-command vlock in /etc/tmux.conf. (3) For SSH sessions, ensure ClientAliveCountMax 0 terminates (not just suspends) idle sessions. (4) Verify: lock a session and confirm no previous content is visible. The system administrator must ensure that all session lock mechanisms properly clear or cover the screen contents.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check verified that the XCP-ng hypervisor automatically monitors remote access methods. The check examined auditd rules for SSH connection logging, rsyslog configuration for auth/authpriv facilities, /var/log/secure contents for SSH authentication records, and /var/log/xensource.log for xapi remote access events. Audit rules are configured to capture SSH connection events, and syslog is logging authentication events to /var/log/secure. The xapi audit trail records remote management connections. This comprehensive monitoring ensures all remote access methods are automatically tracked, meeting the VMM SRG requirement for automatic remote access monitoring.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check found that remote access monitoring is not properly configured on the XCP-ng hypervisor. The VMM SRG requires automatic monitoring of all remote access methods. To remediate: (1) Ensure auditd monitors SSH: add audit rules for /usr/sbin/sshd and network connection events. (2) Configure rsyslog to log auth events: ensure 'auth,authpriv.* /var/log/secure' is in /etc/rsyslog.conf. (3) Enable verbose SSH logging: set LogLevel VERBOSE in /etc/ssh/sshd_config. (4) Verify xapi logging: check /var/log/xensource.log for remote connection records. (5) Restart services: systemctl restart auditd rsyslog sshd. The ISSO/ISSM must ensure remote access logs are reviewed regularly and forwarded to a central log server.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment:
XCP-ng Dom0 uses DoD-approved encryption for remote access session confidentiality.
The automated check verified: (1) SSH is configured with FIPS-approved encryption algorithms (AES-256, AES-128 in CTR or GCM modes), (2) XAPI communicates over HTTPS/TLS providing encrypted API sessions, (3) No weak or deprecated ciphers (DES, 3DES, RC4) are enabled for SSH.
All remote management of XCP-ng occurs via encrypted SSH sessions or XAPI HTTPS connections, ensuring confidentiality of management data in transit. This meets the VMM SRG requirement for DoD-approved encryption of remote access sessions.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment:
XCP-ng Dom0 does not use DoD-approved encryption for all remote access sessions. The automated check found weak or non-FIPS-approved ciphers in SSH configuration, or XAPI HTTPS is not properly configured.
Remediation: 1. Edit /etc/ssh/sshd_config to set approved ciphers: Ciphers aes256-ctr,aes192-ctr,aes128-ctr,aes256-gcm@openssh.com,aes128-gcm@openssh.com 2. Restart SSH: systemctl restart sshd 3. Verify XAPI TLS: openssl s_client -connect localhost:443 4. Remove any non-encrypted remote access methods
Contact the system administrator to harden SSH encryption.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment:
XCP-ng Dom0 produces audit records containing event type information.
The automated check verified that auditd is active and audit records contain type= fields (SYSCALL, PATH, CWD, EXECVE, USER_LOGIN, etc.) establishing what type of events occurred. The Linux audit subsystem automatically classifies each event by type. This meets the VMM SRG requirement.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment:
XCP-ng Dom0 does not produce audit records with event type information.
The automated check found that auditd is not active, not properly configured, or required audit rules are missing.
Remediation: 1. Install auditd if missing: yum install audit 2. Enable and start: systemctl enable auditd; service auditd start 3. Configure required audit rules in /etc/audit/rules.d/ 4. Reload rules: augenrules --load 5. Verify: auditctl -l
Contact the system administrator to configure the audit subsystem.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment:
XCP-ng Dom0 produces audit records with date/time timestamps.
The automated check verified that auditd is active and records contain msg=audit(EPOCH:SERIAL) timestamps providing precise date and time for each event. The kernel audit subsystem generates timestamps from the system clock. This meets the VMM SRG requirement.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment:
XCP-ng Dom0 does not produce properly timestamped audit records.

XCP-ng Dom0 generates audit records for privileged commands.

XCP-ng Dom0 does not fully audit privileged commands.

Answer.ValidTrueComment:
XCP-ng Dom0 alerts administrators on audit processing failures.
The automated check verified that /etc/audit/auditd.conf contains action_mail_acct, space_left_action, and admin_space_left_action settings to alert the SA and ISSO when audit processing fails (disk full, daemon crash). This meets the VMM SRG requirement.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment:
XCP-ng Dom0 does not properly alert on audit processing failures.
The automated check found that auditd.conf does not configure failure alerting.
Remediation: 1. Edit /etc/audit/auditd.conf: action_mail_acct = root space_left_action = email admin_space_left_action = halt 2. Restart auditd: service auditd restart 3. Configure mail forwarding to ISSO/SA
Contact the system administrator to configure audit failure alerting.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment:
XCP-ng Dom0 supports centralized audit record review and analysis.
The automated check verified that aureport and ausearch tools are available, providing capability to review, correlate, and analyze audit records from all system components. These tools support multiple output formats and cross-component analysis. This meets the VMM SRG requirement.

XCP-ng Dom0 does not support centralized audit review capability.
The automated check found that auditd is not active, not properly configured, or required audit rules are missing.
Remediation: 1. Install auditd if missing: yum install audit 2. Enable and start: systemctl enable auditd; service auditd start 3. Configure required audit rules in /etc/audit/rules.d/ 4. Reload rules: augenrules --load 5. Verify: auditctl -l

XCP-ng Dom0 supports filtering audit records by all fields.
The automated check verified that ausearch supports filtering by user (-ua), type (-m), syscall (-sc), key (-k), time range (-ts/-te), and all other audit fields within records. This meets the VMM SRG requirement.

Remediation: 1. Install auditd if missing: yum install audit 2. Enable and start: systemctl enable auditd; service auditd start 3. Configure required audit rules in /etc/audit/rules.d/ 4. Reload rules: augenrules --load 5. Verify: auditctl -l
