Answer.ValidTrueComment:
XCP-ng Dom0 uses internal system clocks for audit record timestamps.
The automated check verified that auditd timestamps are generated from the system clock, and time synchronization is configured via chronyd or ntpd to maintain accurate time. This meets the VMM SRG requirement.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment:
XCP-ng Dom0 does not properly use system clocks for audit timestamps.
The automated check found that time synchronization (chronyd/ntpd) is not configured or not active.
Remediation: 1. Install chronyd: yum install chrony 2. Configure NTP servers in /etc/chrony.conf 3. Enable: systemctl enable chronyd; systemctl start chronyd 4. Verify: chronyc sources
Contact the system administrator to configure time synchronization.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment:
XCP-ng Dom0 protects audit information from unauthorized read access.
The automated check verified that /var/log/audit/ files have permissions 600 or more restrictive, owned by root. Only the root account can read audit log files. This meets the VMM SRG requirement.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment:
XCP-ng Dom0 does not protect audit information from unauthorized read access.
The automated check found audit log files with overly permissive read access.
Remediation: 1. Set audit log permissions: chmod 600 /var/log/audit/audit.log 2. Set ownership: chown root:root /var/log/audit/audit.log 3. Configure auditd.conf: log_group = root 4. Verify: stat -c "%a %U %G" /var/log/audit/*
Contact the system administrator to restrict audit log permissions.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment:
XCP-ng Dom0 protects audit information from unauthorized modification.
The automated check verified that /var/log/audit/ files have no group or world write permissions, and are owned by root. Only the auditd process (running as root) can write to audit logs. This meets the VMM SRG requirement.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment:
XCP-ng Dom0 does not protect audit information from unauthorized modification.
The automated check found audit log files with group or world write permissions.
Remediation: 1. Remove unauthorized write: chmod go-w /var/log/audit/audit.log 2. Set ownership: chown root:root /var/log/audit/audit.log 3. Verify: stat -c "%a %U %G" /var/log/audit/*
Contact the system administrator to restrict audit log write permissions.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment:
XCP-ng Dom0 protects audit information from unauthorized deletion.
The automated check verified that the /var/log/audit/ directory is owned by root with restricted permissions, preventing non-root users from deleting audit log files. auditd manages log rotation (max_log_file_action) to ensure controlled log lifecycle. This meets the VMM SRG requirement.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment:
XCP-ng Dom0 does not protect audit information from unauthorized deletion.
The automated check found /var/log/audit/ directory permissions allow unauthorized deletion.
Remediation: 1. Set directory permissions: chmod 700 /var/log/audit 2. Set ownership: chown root:root /var/log/audit 3. Configure log retention in auditd.conf: max_log_file_action = keep_logs 4. Verify: stat -c "%a %U %G" /var/log/audit
Contact the system administrator to protect audit logs from deletion.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment:
XCP-ng Dom0 provides audit record generation for all VMM components.
The automated check verified that auditd is configured with rules covering system calls, file access, user authentication, privilege escalation, and administrative commands across all VMM components (XAPI, Xen, Dom0 services). This meets the VMM SRG requirement for comprehensive audit generation.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment:
XCP-ng Dom0 does not generate audit records for all VMM component events.
The automated check found that auditd is not active, not properly configured, or required audit rules are missing.
Remediation: 1. Install auditd if missing: yum install audit 2. Enable and start: systemctl enable auditd; service auditd start 3. Configure required audit rules in /etc/audit/rules.d/ 4. Reload rules: augenrules --load 5. Verify: auditctl -l
Contact the system administrator to configure the audit subsystem.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment:
XCP-ng Dom0 restricts audit event selection to the ISSM (or authorized individuals).
The automated check verified that audit rule configuration files in /etc/audit/rules.d/ are owned by root with permissions restricting write access to root only. Only root (the ISSM-appointed administrator) can select which events are audited. This meets the VMM SRG requirement.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment:
XCP-ng Dom0 does not properly restrict audit event configuration to authorized administrators.
The automated check found audit rule files with overly permissive access.
Remediation: 1. Set permissions: chmod 640 /etc/audit/rules.d/*.rules 2. Set ownership: chown root:root /etc/audit/rules.d/*.rules 3. Verify: ls -la /etc/audit/rules.d/
Contact the ISSO to review audit rule management access.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment:
XCP-ng Dom0 generates audit records for privilege access attempts.
The automated check verified that auditd rules monitor privilege escalation events (sudo, su, setuid binaries) capturing both successful and unsuccessful attempts. This meets the VMM SRG requirement.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment:
XCP-ng Dom0 does not audit privilege access attempts.
The automated check found that auditd is not active, not properly configured, or required audit rules are missing.
Remediation: 1. Install auditd if missing: yum install audit 2. Enable and start: systemctl enable auditd; service auditd start 3. Configure required audit rules in /etc/audit/rules.d/ 4. Reload rules: augenrules --load 5. Verify: auditctl -l
Contact the system administrator to configure the audit subsystem.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment:
XCP-ng validates PKI certificates by constructing certification paths to accepted trust anchors.
The automated check verified that the system CA certificate bundle (/etc/pki/tls/certs/ca-bundle.crt) is present and contains DoD Root CA certificates. OpenSSL and XAPI use this bundle for certificate validation. This meets the VMM SRG requirement.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment:
XCP-ng does not properly validate PKI certificates to an accepted trust anchor.
The automated check found the CA certificate bundle is missing or does not contain required DoD Root CA certificates.
Remediation: 1. Install DoD Root CA certificates: cp DoD_Root_CA.pem /etc/pki/ca-trust/source/anchors/ 2. Update CA trust: update-ca-trust 3. Verify: openssl verify -CAfile /etc/pki/tls/certs/ca-bundle.crt /path/to/cert.pem
Contact the PKI administrator to install DoD Root CA certificates.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment:
XCP-ng enforces authorized access to PKI private keys.
The automated check verified that XAPI private key (/etc/xensource/xapi-ssl.pem) and SSH host keys (/etc/ssh/ssh_host_*_key) are owned by root with permissions 600 or more restrictive, preventing unauthorized access. This meets the VMM SRG requirement.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment:
XCP-ng does not properly protect PKI private keys.
The automated check found private key files with overly permissive access.
Remediation: 1. Set XAPI key permissions: chmod 600 /etc/xensource/xapi-ssl.pem; chown root:root /etc/xensource/xapi-ssl.pem 2. Set SSH key permissions: chmod 600 /etc/ssh/ssh_host_*_key; chown root:root /etc/ssh/ssh_host_*_key 3. Verify: stat -c "%a %U" /etc/xensource/xapi-ssl.pem /etc/ssh/ssh_host_*_key
Contact the system administrator to restrict private key access.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment:
XCP-ng maps PKI-authenticated identities to user/group accounts.
The automated check verified that SSH is configured to use PKI certificates for authentication (AuthorizedKeysFile configured, PubkeyAuthentication enabled) and that certificate-based identities map to local or LDAP/AD user accounts. This meets the VMM SRG requirement.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment:
XCP-ng does not properly map PKI-authenticated identities to accounts.
The automated check found that PKI-based authentication mapping is not configured.
Remediation: 1. Enable public key authentication in /etc/ssh/sshd_config: PubkeyAuthentication yes 2. Configure authorized keys: AuthorizedKeysFile .ssh/authorized_keys 3. For AD integration, configure SSSD with certificate mapping 4. Restart sshd: systemctl restart sshd
Contact the system administrator and PKI administrator to configure identity mapping.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment:
XCP-ng Dom0 enforces password complexity requiring at least one uppercase character.
The automated check verified that PAM pwquality/pam_cracklib is configured with ucredit=-1 (or lower) in /etc/security/pwquality.conf or /etc/pam.d/system-auth. This meets the VMM SRG requirement.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment:
XCP-ng Dom0 does not require uppercase characters in passwords.
The automated check found ucredit is not set to -1 or lower.
Remediation: 1. Edit /etc/security/pwquality.conf: ucredit = -1 2. Verify: grep ucredit /etc/security/pwquality.conf
Contact the system administrator to configure password complexity.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment:
XCP-ng Dom0 enforces password complexity requiring at least one lowercase character.
The automated check verified that PAM pwquality/pam_cracklib is configured with lcredit=-1 (or lower) in /etc/security/pwquality.conf or /etc/pam.d/system-auth. This meets the VMM SRG requirement.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment:
XCP-ng Dom0 does not require lowercase characters in passwords.
The automated check found lcredit is not set to -1 or lower.
Remediation: 1. Edit /etc/security/pwquality.conf: lcredit = -1 2. Verify: grep lcredit /etc/security/pwquality.conf
Contact the system administrator to configure password complexity.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment:
XCP-ng Dom0 enforces password complexity requiring at least one numeric character.
The automated check verified that PAM pwquality/pam_cracklib is configured with dcredit=-1 (or lower) in /etc/security/pwquality.conf or /etc/pam.d/system-auth. This meets the VMM SRG requirement.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment:
XCP-ng Dom0 does not require numeric characters in passwords.
The automated check found dcredit is not set to -1 or lower.
Remediation: 1. Edit /etc/security/pwquality.conf: dcredit = -1 2. Verify: grep dcredit /etc/security/pwquality.conf
Contact the system administrator to configure password complexity.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment:
XCP-ng Dom0 requires at least 8 characters to change when passwords are modified.
The automated check verified that PAM pwquality is configured with difok=8 (or higher) in /etc/security/pwquality.conf. This meets the VMM SRG requirement.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment:
XCP-ng Dom0 does not require sufficient character changes in new passwords.
The automated check found difok is not set to 8 or higher.
Remediation: 1. Edit /etc/security/pwquality.conf: difok = 8 2. Verify: grep difok /etc/security/pwquality.conf
Contact the system administrator to configure password change requirements.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment:
XCP-ng Dom0 stores only encrypted representations of passwords.
The automated check verified that /etc/shadow contains hashed passwords (SHA-512 $6$ prefix or similar), no plaintext passwords exist, and /etc/passwd does not contain password hashes (x placeholder used). This meets the VMM SRG requirement.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment:
XCP-ng Dom0 does not properly encrypt stored passwords.
The automated check found plaintext or weakly hashed passwords.
Remediation: 1. Ensure SHA-512 hashing: authconfig --passalgo=sha512 --update 2. Verify shadow entries use $6$ prefix: grep "^root" /etc/shadow 3. Force password changes for affected accounts: chage -d 0 username
Contact the system administrator to configure password hashing.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment:
XCP-ng Dom0 transmits only encrypted representations of passwords.
The automated check verified that SSH encrypts all authentication traffic, XAPI uses HTTPS/TLS for API authentication, and no unencrypted authentication protocols (telnet, FTP, HTTP) are enabled. This meets the VMM SRG requirement.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment:
XCP-ng Dom0 may transmit passwords in unencrypted form.
The automated check found unencrypted services or protocols that could transmit passwords in cleartext.
Remediation: 1. Disable telnet: systemctl disable telnet.socket; systemctl stop telnet.socket 2. Disable FTP: systemctl disable vsftpd; systemctl stop vsftpd 3. Ensure SSH is the only remote access method 4. Verify XAPI uses HTTPS only
Contact the system administrator to disable unencrypted authentication protocols.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment:
XCP-ng Dom0 enforces a 24-hour minimum password lifetime.
The automated check verified that /etc/login.defs contains PASS_MIN_DAYS set to 1 or greater, preventing users from changing passwords more than once per day. This meets the VMM SRG requirement.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment:
XCP-ng Dom0 does not enforce a minimum password lifetime.
The automated check found PASS_MIN_DAYS is not set to 1 or greater in /etc/login.defs.
Remediation: 1. Edit /etc/login.defs: PASS_MIN_DAYS 1 2. Apply to existing accounts: chage --mindays 1 username 3. Verify: grep PASS_MIN_DAYS /etc/login.defs
Contact the system administrator to configure password lifetime.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment:
XCP-ng Dom0 enforces a 60-day maximum password lifetime.
The automated check verified that /etc/login.defs contains PASS_MAX_DAYS set to 60 or less. This meets the VMM SRG requirement.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment:
XCP-ng Dom0 does not enforce a maximum password lifetime.
The automated check found PASS_MAX_DAYS is not set to 60 or less in /etc/login.defs.
Remediation: 1. Edit /etc/login.defs: PASS_MAX_DAYS 60 2. Apply to existing accounts: chage --maxdays 60 username 3. Verify: grep PASS_MAX_DAYS /etc/login.defs
Contact the system administrator to configure password expiration.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment:
XCP-ng Dom0 enforces a minimum 15-character password length.
The automated check verified that PAM pwquality is configured with minlen=15 (or higher) in /etc/security/pwquality.conf or /etc/pam.d/system-auth. This meets the VMM SRG requirement.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment:
XCP-ng Dom0 does not enforce a 15-character minimum password length.
The automated check found minlen is not set to 15 or higher.
Remediation: 1. Edit /etc/security/pwquality.conf: minlen = 15 2. Verify: grep minlen /etc/security/pwquality.conf
Contact the system administrator to configure password length requirements.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment:
XCP-ng Dom0 obscures authentication feedback during the authentication process.
The automated check verified that SSH is configured to not display password characters during entry, and PAM does not echo credentials. The terminal does not display typed password characters, preventing shoulder-surfing attacks. This meets the VMM SRG requirement.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment:
XCP-ng Dom0 does not properly obscure authentication feedback.
The automated check found configuration issues that may expose authentication information during the login process.
Remediation: 1. Verify SSH does not echo passwords (default behavior) 2. Check PAM configuration does not include pam_echo modules 3. Verify console login obscures password entry
Contact the system administrator to verify authentication feedback handling.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment:
XCP-ng enforces approved authorizations for logical access via XAPI RBAC.
The automated check verified: (1) RBAC roles are defined and available via xe role-list, (2) Access to XAPI functions is controlled by role assignment, (3) Non-admin roles (vm-operator, read-only) provide least-privilege access. XAPI RBAC enforces authorization policies for all management operations. This meets the VMM SRG requirement.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment:
XCP-ng does not properly enforce logical access authorizations.
The automated check found RBAC is not configured or all users have pool-admin access.
Remediation: 1. Configure RBAC roles: xe role-list 2. Assign least-privilege roles: xe subject-add subject-name=user role-name=vm-operator 3. Remove unnecessary pool-admin access 4. Verify: xe subject-list
Contact the ISSO to review and approve RBAC role assignments.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment:
XCP-ng is configured to disable non-essential capabilities.
The automated check verified that unnecessary services are disabled on Dom0, non-essential network ports are not listening, and only required XAPI/SSH services are active. This meets the VMM SRG requirement.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment:
XCP-ng has non-essential capabilities enabled.
The automated check found unnecessary services running or non-essential ports listening on Dom0.
Remediation: 1. List active services: systemctl list-units --type=service --state=running 2. Disable non-essential services: systemctl disable SERVICE; systemctl stop SERVICE 3. Review listening ports: ss -tlnp 4. Document required services in the System Security Plan
Contact the system administrator to disable non-essential services.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment:
XCP-ng is configured to restrict functions, ports, protocols, and services per PPSM CAL.
The automated check verified that Dom0 listening ports are limited to essential services (SSH/22, XAPI/443, XAPI/80-redirect), and iptables/firewall rules restrict unauthorized access. This meets the VMM SRG requirement.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment:
XCP-ng does not properly restrict ports, protocols, and services.
The automated check found unauthorized ports listening or insufficient firewall rules.
Remediation: 1. Review listening ports: ss -tlnp 2. Configure iptables to restrict access to authorized ports only 3. Disable unnecessary services 4. Document authorized ports in POA&M per PPSM CAL
Contact the ISSO to review authorized ports and protocols.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment:
XCP-ng uniquely identifies and authenticates organizational users.
The automated check verified that each user has a unique account (no shared accounts detected), UID 0 is limited to root only, and PAM enforces authentication for all user sessions. This meets the VMM SRG requirement.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment:
XCP-ng does not properly enforce unique user identification.
The automated check found shared accounts, duplicate UIDs, or authentication bypass.
Remediation: 1. Review accounts: cat /etc/passwd | awk -F: "{print $3}" | sort | uniq -d 2. Remove shared accounts and create individual accounts 3. Ensure only root has UID 0 4. Configure PAM to require authentication
Contact the system administrator to implement unique user accounts.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment:
XCP-ng uses multifactor authentication for network access to privileged accounts.
The automated check verified the configuration of SSH key-based authentication combined with password/token authentication, or external AD/LDAP integration providing MFA. This meets the VMM SRG requirement when properly configured.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment:
XCP-ng does not implement multifactor authentication for network privileged access.
The automated check found that MFA is not configured for privileged network access. XCP-ng Dom0 relies on single-factor SSH password authentication by default.
Remediation: 1. Configure SSH key-based authentication as the first factor 2. Integrate with AD/LDAP for centralized MFA: xe pool-enable-external-auth 3. Consider RADIUS/TACACS+ integration for MFA 4. Document MFA implementation in the System Security Plan
Contact the ISSO and system administrator to implement MFA for privileged access.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment:
XCP-ng uses multifactor authentication for network access to non-privileged accounts.
The automated check verified MFA configuration for non-privileged network access via SSH key-based authentication or external AD/LDAP integration. This meets the VMM SRG requirement when properly configured.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment:
XCP-ng does not implement MFA for network non-privileged access.
The automated check found that MFA is not configured for non-privileged network access.
Remediation: 1. Configure SSH key + password for all accounts 2. Integrate with AD/LDAP: xe pool-enable-external-auth 3. Ensure non-privileged RBAC subjects use MFA
Contact the ISSO to implement MFA for non-privileged access.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment:
XCP-ng uses multifactor authentication for local access to privileged accounts.
The automated check verified MFA configuration for local console access to privileged accounts. This meets the VMM SRG requirement when properly configured.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment:
XCP-ng does not implement MFA for local privileged access.
The automated check found that MFA is not configured for local console access to privileged accounts. Physical console access typically uses single-factor password authentication.
Remediation: 1. Configure PAM for local MFA (smart card or token) 2. Restrict physical console access 3. Implement compensating controls (physical security, CCTV) 4. Document in POA&M if MFA is not feasible for local console
Contact the ISSO to implement or document MFA for local access.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment:
XCP-ng uses multifactor authentication for local access to non-privileged accounts.
The automated check verified MFA configuration for local access to non-privileged accounts. This meets the VMM SRG requirement when properly configured.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment:
XCP-ng does not implement MFA for local non-privileged access.
The automated check found MFA is not configured for local non-privileged access.
Remediation: 1. Configure PAM for local MFA 2. Integrate with organizational MFA solution 3. Document in POA&M if not feasible
Contact the ISSO to implement MFA for local non-privileged access.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment:
XCP-ng requires individual authentication before group authenticator use.
The automated check verified that PAM configuration requires individual user authentication (password or key) before any group-based access is granted. Users must authenticate individually before accessing shared or group resources. This meets the VMM SRG requirement.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment:
XCP-ng does not require individual authentication before group authenticator use.
The automated check found that group access may be granted without prior individual authentication.
Remediation: 1. Ensure PAM requires individual authentication first 2. Remove any group-only authentication paths 3. Verify sudo requires password: grep NOPASSWD /etc/sudoers (should be empty or justified)
Contact the system administrator to enforce individual authentication.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment:
XCP-ng implements replay-resistant authentication for network privileged access.
The automated check verified that SSH uses protocol 2 with challenge-response or public key authentication, both of which are inherently replay-resistant. XAPI sessions use unique tokens that cannot be replayed. This meets the VMM SRG requirement.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment:
XCP-ng does not implement replay-resistant authentication for network privileged access.
The automated check found authentication mechanisms that may be vulnerable to replay attacks.
Remediation: 1. Ensure SSH Protocol 2 only: Protocol 2 in sshd_config 2. Use SSH key-based authentication 3. Disable legacy protocols vulnerable to replay 4. Verify XAPI uses session tokens with timeout
Contact the system administrator to configure replay-resistant authentication.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment:
XCP-ng implements replay-resistant authentication for network non-privileged access.
The automated check verified that SSH protocol 2 is used for all network authentication, providing inherent replay resistance through cryptographic nonces and session keys. This meets the VMM SRG requirement.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment:
XCP-ng does not implement replay-resistant authentication for network non-privileged access.
The automated check found authentication mechanisms vulnerable to replay.
Remediation: 1. Ensure SSH Protocol 2: Protocol 2 in sshd_config 2. Disable password-only authentication where possible 3. Use key-based authentication
Contact the system administrator to configure replay-resistant authentication.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment:
XCP-ng uniquely identifies peripherals before establishing connections.
The automated check verified that XAPI manages all device assignments and Dom0 identifies devices by PCI bus address, USB vendor/product ID, or similar unique identifiers before allowing VM connections. This meets the VMM SRG requirement.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment:
XCP-ng does not properly identify peripherals before establishing connections.
The automated check found that device identification is not properly enforced before VM assignment.
Remediation: 1. Review device assignments: xe pusb-list; xe pci-list 2. Ensure all device passthrough is explicitly authorized 3. Document authorized devices in the System Security Plan
Contact the system administrator to review peripheral identification.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment:
XCP-ng disables local account identifiers after 35 days of inactivity.
The automated check verified that /etc/default/useradd INACTIVE is set to 35 or less, and existing accounts have appropriate inactivity limits configured via chage. This meets the VMM SRG requirement.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment:
XCP-ng does not disable inactive accounts after 35 days.
The automated check found INACTIVE is not set to 35 or less in /etc/default/useradd, or existing accounts lack inactivity limits.
Remediation: 1. Set default: useradd -D -f 35 2. Apply to existing accounts: chage -I 35 username 3. Verify: useradd -D | grep INACTIVE; chage -l username
Contact the system administrator to configure account inactivity limits.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment:
XCP-ng uses mechanisms meeting federal requirements for authentication to a cryptographic module.
The automated check verified the status of FIPS mode on the system (crypto-policies or /proc/sys/crypto/fips_enabled). This meets the VMM SRG requirement when FIPS mode is enabled.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment:
XCP-ng does not meet federal cryptographic module authentication requirements.
The automated check found FIPS mode is not enabled. XCP-ng (CentOS 7-based) may not fully support FIPS 140-2 validated cryptographic modules.
Remediation: 1. Enable FIPS mode: fips-mode-setup --enable (if supported) 2. Verify: cat /proc/sys/crypto/fips_enabled (should be 1) 3. If FIPS mode is not supported, document as a compliance gap in POA&M 4. Use FIPS-validated crypto libraries where available
Contact the ISSO to document FIPS compliance status.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment:
XCP-ng supports audit reduction and on-demand reporting.
The automated check verified that aureport provides summary reports (--summary, --auth, --login, --event) and ausearch provides on-demand queries with flexible filtering. These tools meet the VMM SRG requirement for audit reduction and reporting capability.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment:
XCP-ng does not support audit reduction and on-demand reporting.
The automated check found that audit reporting tools are not available or functional.
Remediation: 1. Install audit tools: yum install audit 2. Verify aureport: aureport --summary 3. Verify ausearch: ausearch -m USER_LOGIN 4. Configure scheduled reports if required
Contact the system administrator to install audit reporting tools.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment:
XCP-ng automatically removes or disables emergency accounts after the crisis is resolved or within 72 hours.
The automated check verified that emergency/temporary accounts have expiration dates set via chage, and /etc/default/useradd EXPIRE is configured for automatic account expiration. This meets the VMM SRG requirement.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment:
XCP-ng does not automatically remove or disable emergency accounts within 72 hours.
The automated check found accounts without expiration dates or emergency account policies not enforced.
Remediation: 1. Set expiration on emergency accounts: chage -E $(date -d "+3 days" +%Y-%m-%d) emergency_user 2. Configure default expiration: useradd -D -e DATE 3. Create procedures for emergency account lifecycle 4. Review accounts regularly: chage -l username
Contact the ISSO to establish emergency account procedures.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment:
XCP-ng employs strong authenticators for nonlocal maintenance and diagnostic sessions.
The automated check verified that SSH uses strong authentication (key-based or password with complexity requirements) for all remote maintenance sessions, and XAPI requires authenticated HTTPS sessions. This meets the VMM SRG requirement.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment:
XCP-ng does not employ strong authenticators for nonlocal maintenance.
The automated check found weak authentication for remote maintenance sessions.
Remediation: 1. Configure SSH key-based authentication: PubkeyAuthentication yes 2. Disable password-only auth for maintenance: PasswordAuthentication no 3. Ensure strong password policy via PAM 4. Verify XAPI uses HTTPS with authentication
Contact the system administrator to strengthen remote maintenance authentication.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment:
XCP-ng separates user functionality from VMM management functionality.
The automated check verified that Dom0 (management domain) is isolated from DomU (guest VMs) by the Xen hypervisor. User workloads run in DomU while VMM management occurs exclusively in Dom0. The Xen architecture inherently provides this separation through hardware-enforced privilege rings. This meets the VMM SRG requirement.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment:
XCP-ng does not properly separate user and management functionality.
The automated check found potential issues with Dom0/DomU isolation.
Remediation: 1. Verify no user workloads run in Dom0 2. Ensure all user applications run in DomU guest VMs 3. Restrict Dom0 access to authorized administrators only 4. Review Dom0 services: systemctl list-units --type=service
Contact the system administrator to verify Dom0/DomU separation.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment:
XCP-ng isolates security functions from non-security functions.
The automated check verified that the Xen hypervisor provides hardware-enforced isolation between security functions (Dom0, XAPI, authentication) and non-security functions (DomU guest workloads). Security-critical processes run in the privileged Dom0 domain. This meets the VMM SRG requirement.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment:
XCP-ng does not properly isolate security functions.
The automated check found security functions may not be adequately isolated from non-security functions.
Remediation: 1. Ensure security services run only in Dom0 2. Do not install non-essential software in Dom0 3. Restrict Dom0 to management functions only 4. Document security function isolation in the System Security Plan
Contact the ISSO to review security function isolation.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment:
XCP-ng prevents unauthorized information transfer via shared system resources.
The automated check verified that the Xen hypervisor enforces memory isolation between VMs, CPU cache flushing mitigations are applied, and shared storage is access-controlled via XAPI. Dom0 mediates all shared resource access. This meets the VMM SRG requirement.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment:
XCP-ng does not prevent unauthorized information transfer via shared resources.
The automated check found potential shared resource isolation issues.
Remediation: 1. Verify Xen security patches are current: yum update xen 2. Enable CPU side-channel mitigations in GRUB: spec-ctrl=yes 3. Review shared storage access: xe sr-list; xe vdi-list 4. Ensure VMs do not share memory pages unintentionally
Contact the system administrator to verify shared resource isolation.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment:
XCP-ng manages excess capacity to limit DoS attack effects.
The automated check verified that TCP SYN cookies are enabled (net.ipv4.tcp_syncookies=1), connection tracking limits are configured, and XCP-ng resource management (memory/CPU limits per VM) prevents individual VMs from consuming all host resources. This meets the VMM SRG requirement.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment:
XCP-ng does not properly manage capacity for DoS protection.
The automated check found TCP SYN cookies are not enabled, reducing flood protection.
Remediation: 1. Enable SYN cookies: sysctl -w net.ipv4.tcp_syncookies=1 2. Make persistent: echo "net.ipv4.tcp_syncookies = 1" >> /etc/sysctl.d/99-dos.conf 3. Configure connection tracking: sysctl -w net.netfilter.nf_conntrack_max=65536 4. Set VM resource limits via XO or xe CLI
Contact the system administrator to configure DoS protection.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment:
XCP-ng terminates network sessions after specified inactivity periods.
The automated check verified SSH session timeout configuration (ClientAliveInterval and ClientAliveCountMax in sshd_config) and XAPI session timeout settings. Privileged sessions terminate after 10 minutes and user sessions after 15 minutes of inactivity. This meets the VMM SRG requirement.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment:
XCP-ng does not terminate sessions after the required inactivity periods.
The automated check found SSH or XAPI session timeouts are not properly configured.
Remediation: 1. Edit /etc/ssh/sshd_config: ClientAliveInterval 600 ClientAliveCountMax 0 2. Restart sshd: systemctl restart sshd 3. Configure XAPI session timeout in XO settings
Contact the system administrator to configure session timeouts.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment:
XCP-ng fails to a secure state on system initialization, shutdown, or abort failures.
The automated check verified that kernel panic behavior is configured (kernel.panic sysctl), Dom0 crash dump settings capture failure state, and Xen watchdog is configured to handle hypervisor failures. This meets the VMM SRG requirement.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment:
XCP-ng does not properly fail to a secure state.
The automated check found that fail-secure behavior is not fully configured.
Remediation: 1. Configure panic behavior: sysctl -w kernel.panic=10 2. Enable kdump for crash analysis: systemctl enable kdump 3. Configure Xen watchdog timeout 4. Verify: sysctl kernel.panic; systemctl is-enabled kdump
Contact the system administrator to configure fail-secure behavior.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment:
XCP-ng protects the confidentiality and integrity of information at rest.
The automated check verified LUKS/dm-crypt disk encryption status, file system permissions on sensitive directories, and XCP-ng storage repository access controls. This meets the VMM SRG requirement when encryption is properly configured.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment:
XCP-ng does not fully protect data at rest.
The automated check found that disk encryption is not enabled or file system permissions are insufficient.
Remediation: 1. Enable LUKS encryption for storage repositories 2. Verify file permissions on /etc/xensource/, /var/xapi/ 3. Configure encrypted storage for VM disk images 4. Document encryption status in the System Security Plan
Contact the system administrator and ISSO to implement data-at-rest encryption.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment:
XCP-ng checks the validity of data inputs.
The automated check verified that XAPI validates API inputs through its type system and parameter validation, SSH enforces protocol-level input validation, and web-facing services sanitize inputs. This meets the VMM SRG requirement.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment:
XCP-ng does not properly validate data inputs.
The automated check found potential input validation gaps.
Remediation: 1. Ensure XAPI is current with security patches: yum update xapi 2. Configure SSH to reject invalid inputs 3. Review any custom scripts for input validation 4. Apply vendor security updates
Contact the system administrator to verify input validation controls.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment:
XCP-ng generates error messages with corrective action information without revealing exploitable data.
The automated check verified that system error messages provide sufficient information for corrective action (log files, error codes) without exposing internal system details, stack traces, or configuration paths to unauthorized users. This meets the VMM SRG requirement.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment:
XCP-ng error messages may reveal exploitable information.
The automated check found error messages that expose internal system details.
Remediation: 1. Configure sshd LogLevel to INFO (not DEBUG): LogLevel INFO in sshd_config 2. Review XAPI error handling configuration 3. Ensure error pages do not expose stack traces or paths 4. Restart affected services after changes
Contact the system administrator to review error message configuration.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment:
XCP-ng reveals system error messages only to authorized users.
The automated check verified that system logs are readable only by root (permissions 600 on /var/log/messages, /var/log/secure), XAPI error details require authenticated sessions, and console error output is restricted to logged-in administrators. This meets the VMM SRG requirement.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment:
XCP-ng may reveal system error messages to unauthorized users.
The automated check found log files or error outputs accessible to non-authorized users.
Remediation: 1. Set log permissions: chmod 600 /var/log/messages /var/log/secure 2. Ensure XAPI requires authentication for error details 3. Review console access restrictions 4. Verify: stat -c "%a %U" /var/log/messages /var/log/secure
Contact the system administrator to restrict error message access.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment:
XCP-ng guest VM interactions are mediated by the VMM (Xen hypervisor and XAPI toolstack).
The automated check verified: (1) All VM virtual network interfaces (VIFs) are connected to XAPI-managed virtual bridges, (2) No PCI passthrough devices bypass VMM mediation, (3) All XAPI-managed networks use standard virtual bridge backends (openvswitch or linux bridge), (4) The Xen hypervisor architecture inherently mediates all inter-VM communication through Dom0 network bridges.
XCP-ng enforces hardware-level VM isolation through Xen ring-based privilege separation. Guest VMs (DomU) operate in Ring 3 and cannot directly access hardware or communicate with other VMs without passing through the Dom0 network stack managed by XAPI. This meets the VMM SRG requirement for mediated guest VM interactions.
No additional configuration required.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment:
PCI passthrough devices were detected that bypass VMM mediation for guest VM interactions.
The automated check found one or more VMs with direct PCI device passthrough, which allows guest VMs to access physical hardware directly without XAPI/Xen mediation. This violates the VMM SRG requirement that all VM interactions be mediated by the VMM.
Remediation: 1. Review all PCI passthrough assignments: xe vm-list params=name-label,other-config 2. Remove unauthorized PCI passthrough: xe vm-param-remove param-name=other-config param-key=pci uuid=VM_UUID 3. Document any authorized passthrough devices in the System Security Plan with ISSO/ISSM approval 4. Use XAPI-managed virtual devices (VIFs, VBDs) instead of direct hardware passthrough where possible
Contact the ISSO/ISSM to review and approve any remaining PCI passthrough requirements.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment:
XCP-ng Dom0 automatically audits account modification events through auditd.
The automated check verified: (1) auditd rules monitor /etc/passwd for account attribute changes, (2) auditd rules monitor /etc/shadow for password and account expiry changes, (3) auditd rules monitor /etc/group for group membership modifications, (4) auditd rules monitor /etc/gshadow for group password changes. These four files capture all account modification events on CentOS 7-based XCP-ng Dom0.
Account modifications via usermod, chage, or direct file editing are recorded in the audit log with user identity, timestamp, and change details. This meets the VMM SRG requirement for automatic auditing of account modifications.
No additional configuration required.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment:
One or more critical account files are not monitored by auditd for modification events.
The automated check found that auditd rules do not fully cover account modification files (/etc/passwd, /etc/shadow, /etc/group, /etc/gshadow). Without complete audit coverage, account modifications may not be recorded, violating the VMM SRG requirement.
Remediation: 1. Add audit rules to /etc/audit/rules.d/account-modification.rules: -w /etc/passwd -p wa -k identity -w /etc/shadow -p wa -k identity -w /etc/group -p wa -k identity -w /etc/gshadow -p wa -k identity 2. Reload audit rules: augenrules --load 3. Verify rules are active: auditctl -l | grep identity 4. Restart auditd if needed: service auditd restart
Contact the system administrator to implement these audit rules.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment:
XCP-ng Dom0 automatically audits account disabling actions through auditd.
The automated check verified: (1) auditd rules monitor /etc/shadow for account lock/disable changes (passwd -l, usermod -L), (2) auditd rules monitor /etc/passwd for account shell changes (e.g., setting shell to /sbin/nologin). Account disabling on CentOS 7 modifies these files, and auditd captures all write operations.
When an administrator disables an account using passwd -l or usermod -L, the /etc/shadow file is modified (adding a ! prefix to the password hash), which triggers the auditd watch rule. This provides a complete audit trail of account disabling actions with timestamp, acting user, and affected account.
This meets the VMM SRG requirement for automatic auditing of account disabling actions.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment:
Account disabling actions are not fully audited on XCP-ng Dom0.
The automated check found that auditd does not monitor /etc/shadow and/or /etc/passwd for write operations. Without these rules, account lock/disable events (passwd -l, usermod -L) are not recorded in the audit log.
Remediation: 1. Add audit rules to /etc/audit/rules.d/account-disable.rules: -w /etc/shadow -p wa -k identity -w /etc/passwd -p wa -k identity 2. Reload audit rules: augenrules --load 3. Verify: auditctl -l | grep -E "shadow|passwd" 4. Test by locking a test account and confirming audit log entry: ausearch -k identity
Contact the system administrator to implement these audit rules.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment:
XCP-ng Dom0 automatically audits account removal actions through auditd.
The automated check verified: (1) auditd rules monitor /etc/passwd for user account deletions, (2) auditd rules monitor /etc/group for group removals, (3) auditd rules monitor /etc/shadow for corresponding shadow entry removals. When userdel or groupdel commands are executed, modifications to these three files are captured by auditd watch rules.
The audit trail includes the identity of the administrator who performed the removal, the timestamp, the affected account/group, and the system call details. This provides complete accountability for account removal actions on the XCP-ng hypervisor.
This meets the VMM SRG requirement for automatic auditing of account removal actions.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment:
Account removal actions are not fully audited on XCP-ng Dom0.
The automated check found that auditd does not monitor all three critical account files (/etc/passwd, /etc/group, /etc/shadow). Without complete coverage, account removal events via userdel/groupdel may not be recorded.
Remediation: 1. Add audit rules to /etc/audit/rules.d/account-removal.rules: -w /etc/passwd -p wa -k identity -w /etc/group -p wa -k identity -w /etc/shadow -p wa -k identity 2. Reload audit rules: augenrules --load 3. Verify: auditctl -l | grep identity 4. Test by removing a test account and confirming audit entry: ausearch -k identity
Contact the system administrator to implement the missing audit rules.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment:
All guest VM network communications on XCP-ng use XAPI-provisioned virtual network interfaces (VIFs).
The automated check verified: (1) All VM network interfaces are VIFs connected to XAPI-managed virtual bridges, (2) No PCI NIC passthrough bypasses the virtual network layer, (3) All XAPI-managed networks have corresponding bridge devices in Dom0. XCP-ng provisions all VM networking through XAPI, which creates VIF devices that connect to virtual bridges (xenbr0, xapi0, etc.) managed by the toolstack.
Guest VMs cannot access physical network interfaces directly. All network traffic passes through Dom0's virtual bridge infrastructure, providing full VMM mediation of network communications. This meets the VMM SRG requirement for virtual network device provisioning.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment:
PCI NIC passthrough was detected, allowing VMs to bypass XAPI-provisioned virtual networking.
The automated check found VM configurations with direct PCI NIC passthrough, which gives guest VMs unmediated access to physical network hardware. This bypasses the VMM's virtual network device provisioning and violates the VMM SRG requirement.
Remediation: 1. Identify VMs with PCI NIC passthrough: xe vm-list params=name-label,other-config 2. Replace PCI passthrough NICs with XAPI-managed VIFs: xe vif-create vm-uuid=VM_UUID network-uuid=NET_UUID device=0 3. Remove PCI passthrough configuration from affected VMs 4. If passthrough is operationally required, document in the System Security Plan with ISSO/ISSM approval and include in POA&M
Contact the system administrator to reconfigure VM networking.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment:
All interactions between guest VMs and external systems are mediated by XCP-ng VMM.
The automated check verified: (1) No USB passthrough devices are configured for any VMs, (2) No GPU/vGPU passthrough groups are assigned to VMs, (3) No SR-IOV network configurations bypass VMM mediation. All VM I/O passes through XAPI-managed virtual devices (VBDs for storage, VIFs for networking, virtual console for display).
XCP-ng mediates all VM-external interactions through the Dom0 service VM and XAPI toolstack. Physical devices are accessed only through virtualized device backends in Dom0, ensuring the VMM maintains control over all VM-to-external communications. This meets the VMM SRG requirement for mediated VM-external interactions via interface devices.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment:
Direct hardware passthrough was detected that bypasses VMM mediation for VM-external interactions.
The automated check found USB passthrough, GPU passthrough, or SR-IOV configurations that allow guest VMs direct access to physical hardware without XAPI/Xen mediation. This violates the VMM SRG requirement for mediated VM-external interactions.
Remediation: 1. Review USB passthrough: xe pusb-list 2. Review GPU groups: xe gpu-group-list 3. Review SR-IOV networks: xe network-list params=name-label,other-config 4. Remove unauthorized passthrough assignments 5. Use XAPI-managed virtual devices instead where possible 6. Document any authorized passthrough in the System Security Plan with ISSO/ISSM approval
Contact the system administrator and ISSO to review all hardware passthrough configurations.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment:
XCP-ng implements cryptography to protect remote access session integrity.
The automated check verified: (1) SSH is configured with approved ciphers (no weak ciphers such as 3DES, Arcfour, Blowfish, or CAST128), (2) SSH MACs use HMAC-SHA2 or similar approved algorithms, (3) XAPI communicates over HTTPS/TLS on port 443 with a valid TLS certificate at /etc/xensource/xapi-ssl.pem.
All remote management of XCP-ng occurs via SSH (for Dom0 shell access) or XAPI HTTPS (for Xen Orchestra and xe CLI). Both channels implement cryptographic integrity protection. SSH uses approved ciphers and MACs per DoD requirements. XAPI uses TLS for all API communications. This meets the VMM SRG requirement for cryptographic protection of remote access session integrity.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment:
Remote access sessions do not fully implement approved cryptography for integrity protection.
The automated check found weak SSH ciphers (3DES, Arcfour, Blowfish, or CAST128) or XAPI HTTPS not properly configured on port 443. Weak ciphers do not meet DoD requirements for cryptographic integrity protection.
Remediation: 1. Edit /etc/ssh/sshd_config to remove weak ciphers: Ciphers aes256-ctr,aes192-ctr,aes128-ctr,aes256-gcm@openssh.com,aes128-gcm@openssh.com MACs hmac-sha2-512,hmac-sha2-256 2. Restart sshd: systemctl restart sshd 3. Verify XAPI TLS: openssl s_client -connect localhost:443 4. Regenerate XAPI certificate if expired or self-signed per organizational PKI policy
Contact the system administrator to harden SSH and XAPI TLS configuration.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment:
XCP-ng Dom0 initiates session audits at system startup via auditd.
The automated check verified: (1) auditd service is enabled at boot (systemctl is-enabled auditd = enabled), (2) auditd service is currently active (systemctl is-active auditd = active), (3) Kernel boot parameter audit=1 ensures audit events are captured from early boot before auditd starts.
With auditd enabled at boot, all session-related events (login, logout, session creation, privilege escalation) are captured from system startup. The audit subsystem initializes during the kernel boot process and auditd takes over logging once userspace is ready. This provides continuous audit coverage from boot to shutdown.
This meets the VMM SRG requirement for initiating session audits at system startup.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment:
The auditd service is not properly configured to start at system boot.
The automated check found that auditd is not enabled at boot or is not currently active. Without auditd starting at boot, session events occurring during system startup are not audited, creating a gap in the audit trail.
Remediation: 1. Enable auditd at boot: systemctl enable auditd 2. Start auditd: service auditd start 3. Add audit=1 to kernel boot parameters in /etc/default/grub: GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX="... audit=1" 4. Rebuild grub: grub2-mkconfig -o /boot/grub2/grub.cfg 5. Verify: systemctl is-enabled auditd; systemctl is-active auditd
Contact the system administrator to enable auditd at boot.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment:
XCP-ng Dom0 produces audit records containing identity information for all audited events.
The automated check verified: (1) auditd is active with configured audit rules, (2) Audit records inherently include identity fields: uid (effective user ID), auid (audit/login user ID), pid (process ID), exe (executable path), and subj (SELinux subject context when applicable). These fields are automatically populated by the Linux audit subsystem for every event.
The auid field is particularly important as it preserves the original login identity even after privilege escalation (su/sudo), providing accountability back to the individual who initiated the session. Combined with timestamp and event type, this provides complete identity information for all audited events.
This meets the VMM SRG requirement for audit records containing identity information.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment:
The audit system is not properly configured to produce records with identity information.
The automated check found that auditd is not active or has no configured audit rules. Without an active audit system, no records are produced to establish the identity of individuals or processes associated with security-relevant events.
Remediation: 1. Install auditd if missing: yum install audit 2. Enable and start: systemctl enable auditd; service auditd start 3. Add baseline audit rules: augenrules --load 4. Verify records include identity: ausearch -m USER_LOGIN --start recent 5. Confirm uid, auid, pid fields in audit log: tail /var/log/audit/audit.log
Contact the system administrator to configure the audit subsystem.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment:
XCP-ng Dom0 audit tools are protected from unauthorized access.
The automated check verified permissions on audit binaries: auditctl, aureport, ausearch, autrace, auditd, and augenrules in /sbin/. All binaries are owned by root with permissions no more permissive than 755, preventing unauthorized users from executing privileged audit functions. The audit log file (/var/log/audit/audit.log) is also verified with permissions 600 or more restrictive, owned by root.
These file permissions ensure that only the root account can modify audit tool configurations and that non-privileged users cannot read raw audit logs containing potentially sensitive security event data. The RPM package management system provides additional integrity assurance.
This meets the VMM SRG requirement for protecting audit tools from unauthorized access.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment:
Audit tools have improper access permissions on XCP-ng Dom0.
The automated check found audit binaries or log files with permissions more permissive than required, or not owned by root. This allows unauthorized users to access audit functions or read audit data.
Remediation: 1. Set correct ownership: chown root:root /sbin/auditctl /sbin/aureport /sbin/ausearch /sbin/autrace /sbin/auditd /sbin/augenrules 2. Set correct permissions: chmod 750 /sbin/auditctl /sbin/aureport /sbin/ausearch /sbin/autrace /sbin/auditd /sbin/augenrules 3. Protect audit log: chmod 600 /var/log/audit/audit.log; chown root:root /var/log/audit/audit.log 4. Verify: stat -c "%a %U %G" /sbin/audit*
Contact the system administrator to correct audit tool permissions.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment:
XCP-ng Dom0 audit tools are protected from unauthorized modification.
The automated check verified: (1) All audit binaries (auditctl, aureport, ausearch, autrace, auditd, augenrules) are owned by root, (2) No group or other write permissions exist on any audit binary, (3) Audit configuration files (/etc/audit/auditd.conf, /etc/audit/audit.rules) are owned by root with no unauthorized write access.
Only the root account can modify audit tool binaries and configuration. Group and world write bits are not set on any audit-related files. The RPM package manager provides additional integrity verification (rpm -V audit) to detect unauthorized modifications.
This meets the VMM SRG requirement for protecting audit tools from unauthorized modification.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment:
Audit tools may be modified by unauthorized users on XCP-ng Dom0.
The automated check found audit binaries or configuration files with group or world write permissions, or not owned by root. This allows non-root users to potentially modify audit tools or configuration.
Remediation: 1. Remove unauthorized write permissions from binaries: chmod go-w /sbin/auditctl /sbin/aureport /sbin/ausearch /sbin/autrace /sbin/auditd /sbin/augenrules 2. Secure configuration files: chmod 640 /etc/audit/auditd.conf /etc/audit/audit.rules; chown root:root /etc/audit/auditd.conf /etc/audit/audit.rules 3. Verify RPM integrity: rpm -V audit 4. Verify: stat -c "%a %U %G" /sbin/audit* /etc/audit/*.conf /etc/audit/*.rules
Contact the system administrator to correct audit tool permissions.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment:
XCP-ng Dom0 audit tools are protected from unauthorized deletion.
The automated check verified: (1) The /sbin directory is owned by root with permissions 755, preventing non-root users from deleting files within it, (2) All audit binaries (auditctl, aureport, ausearch, autrace, auditd, augenrules) are present, (3) Audit tools are RPM-managed (audit package), providing package-level integrity and the ability to restore deleted files via yum reinstall audit.
On CentOS 7-based XCP-ng, the /sbin directory permissions ensure only root can create or delete files. The RPM package management system tracks all audit tool files and can detect or restore unauthorized deletions. This provides defense-in-depth protection against unauthorized removal of audit capabilities.
This meets the VMM SRG requirement for protecting audit tools from unauthorized deletion.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment:
Audit tools may be vulnerable to unauthorized deletion on XCP-ng Dom0.
The automated check found that /sbin directory permissions are not properly restricted, or audit tool binaries are missing. Without proper directory-level protection, non-root users may be able to delete audit tools.
Remediation: 1. Verify /sbin ownership and permissions: chown root:root /sbin; chmod 755 /sbin 2. Reinstall missing audit tools: yum reinstall audit 3. Verify all audit binaries are present: rpm -V audit 4. Consider enabling immutable attribute on critical binaries: chattr +i /sbin/auditd (requires careful change management)
Contact the system administrator to restore audit tool integrity.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment:
XCP-ng limits privileges to change software in VMM library directories.
The automated check verified: (1) /opt/xensource/lib is owned by root with no group/world write permissions, (2) /usr/lib64/xen and /usr/lib/xen are owned by root with restricted permissions, (3) /usr/lib64 system libraries are properly protected, (4) No world-writable files exist within /opt/xensource/lib.
Only the root account can modify VMM software libraries including XAPI plugins, Xen hypervisor modules, and supporting libraries. The RPM package manager provides additional integrity verification for system libraries. This ensures that unauthorized users cannot tamper with VMM software components.
This meets the VMM SRG requirement for limiting privileges to change software in libraries.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment:
Software library directories have overly permissive access on XCP-ng Dom0.
The automated check found VMM library directories or files with group/world write permissions, or world-writable files within /opt/xensource/lib. This allows unauthorized users to modify VMM software components.
Remediation: 1. Fix library directory permissions: chmod 755 /opt/xensource/lib /usr/lib64/xen /usr/lib/xen 2. Fix ownership: chown -R root:root /opt/xensource/lib 3. Remove world-writable files: find /opt/xensource/lib -perm -002 -exec chmod o-w {} \; 4. Verify RPM integrity: rpm -Va | grep lib 5. Verify: find /opt/xensource/lib -maxdepth 3 -perm -002 -type f
Contact the system administrator to restrict library directory permissions.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment:
XCP-ng Dom0 enforces password complexity requiring at least one special character.
The automated check verified: (1) PAM pwquality or pam_cracklib is configured with ocredit=-1 (or lower), requiring at least one special character in passwords, (2) The setting is active in /etc/security/pwquality.conf or /etc/pam.d/system-auth.
The ocredit parameter with a negative value specifies the minimum number of special (other) characters required. A value of -1 requires at least one special character. This is enforced by PAM during password changes via passwd or other PAM-aware utilities.
This meets the VMM SRG requirement for password complexity enforcement with special characters.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment:
XCP-ng Dom0 does not enforce the special character requirement for password complexity.
The automated check found that ocredit is not set to -1 (or lower) in /etc/security/pwquality.conf or PAM configuration. Without this setting, passwords can be created without special characters, weakening password strength.
Remediation: 1. Edit /etc/security/pwquality.conf and set: ocredit = -1 2. Alternatively, add to /etc/pam.d/system-auth: password requisite pam_pwquality.so ... ocredit=-1 3. Verify the setting is active: grep ocredit /etc/security/pwquality.conf 4. Test by attempting to set a password without special characters
Contact the system administrator to configure password complexity requirements.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check verified that XCP-ng preserves system failure information through multiple mechanisms. The kdump service is active and configured with crashkernel boot parameter, which reserves memory for kernel crash dump generation. When a system failure occurs, kdump captures the kernel memory state to /var/crash/ for post-mortem analysis. Additionally, the Xen hypervisor maintains its own crash dump capability through xen-crashdump-analyser and preserves hypervisor console output via dmesg. The /var/log/xensource.log captures XAPI management events leading up to failures. These mechanisms collectively preserve information necessary to determine the cause of system failure and support rapid return to operations. The combination of kernel crash dumps, Xen hypervisor logs, and XAPI event logs provides comprehensive failure analysis capability meeting the VMM SRG requirement for failure information preservation.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check found that XCP-ng is not properly configured to preserve system failure information. The kdump service is either not active or not enabled, or the crashkernel boot parameter is not configured. Without kdump, kernel crash dumps will not be generated during system failures, preventing root cause analysis. To remediate: (1) Install kdump: yum install kexec-tools; (2) Configure crashkernel in GRUB: add crashkernel=auto to kernel parameters in /boot/grub/grub.cfg; (3) Enable and start kdump: systemctl enable --now kdump; (4) Verify kdump is operational: kdumpctl status. The system administrator must ensure crash dump storage has sufficient space and that dumps are reviewed after failures to determine root cause and minimize mission disruption.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check verified that XCP-ng pool is configured with external authentication (AD/LDAP). Active Directory integration provides centralized account management where account creation actions generate notifications through AD event logs (Event ID 4720), Group Policy, and organizational monitoring tools such as SIEM. The SA and ISSO are notified of account creation through the enterprise directory service notification mechanisms. The xe pool-list params=external-auth-type command confirmed external authentication is active. This compensating control meets the VMM SRG requirement for account creation notification by delegating to the enterprise identity management infrastructure which provides built-in notification, audit logging, and approval workflows for account lifecycle events.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check found that XCP-ng pool does not have external authentication (AD/LDAP) configured to provide account creation notifications to SA/ISSO. Without external authentication integration, XCP-ng relies on local account management which does not natively generate notifications when accounts are created. To remediate: (1) Configure external authentication: xe pool-enable-external-auth auth-type=AD service-name=domain.example.com; (2) Configure AD Group Policy to notify SA/ISSO when new accounts are created (Event ID 4720); (3) Alternatively, configure auditd rules with email forwarding: auditctl -w /etc/passwd -p wa -k identity, combined with a mail alias for root to forward to SA/ISSO. The ISSO must ensure organizational notification procedures are documented in the SSP.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check verified that XCP-ng pool is configured with external authentication (AD/LDAP). Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check found that XCP-ng pool does not have external authentication (AD/LDAP) configured to provide account modification notifications. To remediate: (1) Configure external authentication: xe pool-enable-external-auth auth-type=AD service-name=domain.example.com; (2) Configure AD monitoring for account modification events (Event IDs 4738, 4742); (3) Alternatively, configure auditd with mail forwarding for /etc/passwd and /etc/shadow changes. The ISSO must establish notification procedures for account modification events and document them in the System Security Plan.

To remediate: (1) Configure external authentication: xe pool-enable-external-auth auth-type=AD service-name=domain.example.com; (2) Configure AD monitoring for account removal events (Event ID 4726); (3) Alternatively, configure auditd rules monitoring /etc/passwd and /etc/shadow with email forwarding to SA/ISSO. Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check verified that XCP-ng audit tools are protected by RPM package management with GPG signature verification. The audit package (containing auditctl, auditd, ausearch, aureport) is installed via RPM with cryptographic signatures verified against the XCP-ng GPG key. Running rpm -V audit confirms file integrity by comparing installed file checksums (MD5/SHA256) against the RPM database. GPG keys are installed in /etc/pki/rpm-gpg/ for verifying package authenticity. This RPM-based integrity protection ensures that audit tool binaries have not been tampered with, meeting the VMM SRG requirement for cryptographic protection of audit tool integrity.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check found that audit tool integrity protection is not properly configured. Either the audit package is not installed, RPM verification shows file modifications (rpm -V audit returns failures), or GPG keys are not configured for signature verification. To remediate: (1) Install the audit package: yum install audit; (2) Verify package integrity: rpm -V audit (should return no output if all files are intact); (3) Ensure GPG keys are installed: rpm --import /etc/pki/rpm-gpg/RPM-GPG-KEY-xcpng; (4) If files have been modified, reinstall: yum reinstall audit. The system administrator must investigate any audit tool integrity failures before restoring from trusted sources.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check verified that XCP-ng is configured to automatically terminate user sessions after inactivity timeouts. SSH ClientAliveInterval is configured in /etc/ssh/sshd_config to detect and terminate inactive SSH sessions. The ClientAliveCountMax setting limits the number of keepalive probes before disconnection. Additionally, the TMOUT shell variable is set in /etc/profile or /etc/profile.d/ to enforce interactive shell session timeouts. These combined mechanisms ensure that both SSH connections and interactive console sessions are terminated after the DoD-defined inactivity period, meeting the VMM SRG requirement for automatic session termination on inactivity.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check found that session inactivity timeouts are not properly configured. SSH ClientAliveInterval is either not set or exceeds the DoD-required maximum (typically 600 seconds/10 minutes). To remediate: (1) Set SSH timeout in /etc/ssh/sshd_config: ClientAliveInterval 600 and ClientAliveCountMax 0; (2) Restart sshd: systemctl restart sshd; (3) Set shell timeout: echo "TMOUT=600; export TMOUT; readonly TMOUT" > /etc/profile.d/tmout.sh; (4) Verify: source /etc/profile.d/tmout.sh. The system administrator must ensure all management interfaces enforce session inactivity timeouts per the organization-defined time period (typically 10-15 minutes for privileged sessions).
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check verified that XCP-ng provides logout capability for user-initiated communications sessions. SSH sessions support exit and logout commands for immediate session termination. The xe CLI provides session-logout command for terminating XAPI management sessions. The sshd service is active, confirming the primary management interface is available with built-in logout functionality. Users can terminate their authenticated sessions at any time through these mechanisms, meeting the VMM SRG requirement for user-initiated session logout capability.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check found that the SSH service is not active on XCP-ng. SSH is the primary management interface providing logout capability through exit/logout commands. Without SSH, users cannot reliably terminate their authenticated sessions. To remediate: (1) Enable SSH: systemctl enable --now sshd; (2) Verify SSH is running: systemctl status sshd; (3) Ensure firewall allows SSH (port 22): iptables -L INPUT -n | grep 22. The system administrator must ensure at least one remote management interface (SSH) is active and provides users the ability to terminate their sessions.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check verified that XCP-ng displays explicit logout messages indicating reliable termination of authenticated sessions. SSH protocol displays "Connection to [host] closed." upon logout, providing explicit confirmation of session termination. The SSH PrintLastLog feature shows login history upon connection. The xe CLI returns success confirmation on session-logout. These mechanisms provide users with reliable indication that their authenticated communication sessions have been properly terminated, meeting the VMM SRG requirement for explicit logout message display.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check could not confirm explicit logout message display on XCP-ng. SSH should be configured to display connection termination messages. To remediate: (1) Ensure SSH is active: systemctl enable --now sshd; (2) Verify PrintLastLog is enabled in /etc/ssh/sshd_config (default: yes); (3) Optionally configure /etc/motd with logout instructions. The SSH protocol inherently displays "Connection to [host] closed." on logout, which satisfies this requirement when SSH is properly configured and active.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check verified that XCP-ng controls remote access methods through SSH (port 22) and XAPI (port 443). SSH is the primary management interface with configurable access restrictions including AllowUsers, AllowGroups, DenyUsers, DenyGroups, and PermitRootLogin directives. XAPI provides the hypervisor management API with TLS encryption on port 443. Both services are active and their access can be restricted through iptables firewall rules. The combination of SSH access controls, XAPI authentication, and iptables filtering provides comprehensive remote access control meeting the VMM SRG requirement.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check found that one or more remote access services are not properly configured on XCP-ng. Both SSH and XAPI must be active for proper hypervisor management. To remediate: (1) Enable SSH: systemctl enable --now sshd; (2) Enable XAPI: systemctl enable --now xapi; (3) Configure SSH access restrictions in /etc/ssh/sshd_config (AllowUsers, AllowGroups); (4) Configure iptables to restrict access by source IP: iptables -A INPUT -s [trusted_network] -p tcp --dport 22 -j ACCEPT; iptables -A INPUT -s [trusted_network] -p tcp --dport 443 -j ACCEPT.
