The system administrator must ensure only authorized networks can access management interfaces.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check verified that XCP-ng provides multiple mechanisms to immediately disconnect or disable remote access: (1) systemctl stop sshd terminates all SSH connections immediately; (2) iptables rules can block all incoming traffic (iptables -A INPUT -j DROP); (3) xe session-logout terminates individual XAPI sessions on demand; (4) systemctl stop xapi disables management API access. These mechanisms allow administrators to immediately disconnect remote access in response to security incidents. The iptables utility is available on the system, confirming firewall-based disconnection capability. This meets the VMM SRG requirement for immediate remote access disconnection.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check found that iptables or other firewall tools are not available on XCP-ng for immediate remote access disconnection. Without firewall tools, administrators cannot immediately block all remote access during security incidents. To remediate: (1) Install iptables: yum install iptables-services; (2) Enable iptables service: systemctl enable iptables; (3) Verify iptables is functional: iptables -L. Administrators should prepare emergency disconnection procedures: iptables -A INPUT -j DROP (blocks all traffic) or systemctl stop sshd && systemctl stop xapi (stops management services). Document these procedures in the incident response plan.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check determined that this requirement is Not Applicable. XCP-ng is a server-class Type 1 hypervisor that runs on rack-mounted or tower server hardware without wireless networking interfaces. The check confirmed no wireless interfaces exist in /sys/class/net/*/wireless and no wireless kernel modules (wifi, iwl, ath, rtl, wlan, 80211) are loaded. All management access to XCP-ng occurs via wired Ethernet connections using SSH (port 22) and XAPI (port 443). Since wireless access is not applicable to this server-class hypervisor platform, the wireless encryption requirement does not apply.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check detected wireless interfaces or wireless kernel modules on this XCP-ng hypervisor host. Wireless interfaces should not be present on server-class hypervisor systems per DoD security requirements. If wireless access must be used, it must be protected with WPA3 or equivalent FIPS-validated encryption. To remediate: (1) Remove wireless kernel modules: modprobe -r [module_name]; (2) Blacklist wireless modules: echo "blacklist [module_name]" > /etc/modprobe.d/blacklist-wireless.conf; (3) Disable any wireless interfaces: ip link set [interface] down; (4) Remove wireless packages if installed: yum remove iw wireless-tools. Wireless interfaces on hypervisor hosts represent an unnecessary attack surface.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check determined that this requirement is Not Applicable. XCP-ng is a server-class Type 1 hypervisor that runs on rack-mounted or tower server hardware without wireless networking interfaces. The check confirmed no wireless interfaces exist in /sys/class/net/*/wireless and no wireless kernel modules are loaded. All management access occurs via wired Ethernet connections using SSH and XAPI with authentication. Since wireless access is not applicable to this server-class hypervisor platform, the wireless authentication requirement does not apply.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check detected wireless interfaces or wireless kernel modules on this XCP-ng hypervisor host. Wireless interfaces should not be present on server-class hypervisor systems. If wireless must be used, it must employ WPA3-Enterprise with 802.1X authentication using DoD-approved certificates. To remediate: (1) Remove wireless kernel modules: modprobe -r [module_name]; (2) Blacklist wireless modules: echo "blacklist [module_name]" > /etc/modprobe.d/blacklist-wireless.conf; (3) Disable wireless interfaces: ip link set [interface] down. Wireless interfaces on hypervisor hosts represent an unnecessary attack surface and should be removed.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check verified that auditd is active and configured to audit account enabling actions on XCP-ng. Audit rules are in place monitoring /etc/passwd and /etc/shadow for write access, which captures account status changes including enabling (unlocking) accounts via usermod -U or passwd commands. When an account is enabled, the file modification is recorded in the audit log with the user, timestamp, and action details. This meets the VMM SRG requirement for automatically auditing account enabling actions through the Linux audit framework.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check found that auditd is not properly configured to audit account enabling actions on XCP-ng. Either the auditd service is not active or audit rules do not monitor the identity files (/etc/passwd, /etc/shadow, /etc/group). To remediate: (1) Enable auditd: systemctl enable --now auditd; (2) Add audit rules: auditctl -w /etc/passwd -p wa -k identity; auditctl -w /etc/shadow -p wa -k identity; auditctl -w /etc/group -p wa -k identity; (3) Make rules persistent in /etc/audit/rules.d/identity.rules; (4) Reload rules: augenrules --load. These rules will capture all account enabling, disabling, creation, and removal events.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check verified that XCP-ng pool is configured with external authentication (AD/LDAP). Active Directory integration provides centralized account management where account enabling actions generate notifications through AD event logs (Event ID 4722 - user account enabled), Group Policy, and organizational SIEM monitoring. The SA and ISSO receive notification of account enabling through the enterprise directory service notification mechanisms. This compensating control meets the VMM SRG requirement for account enabling notification by delegating to the enterprise identity management infrastructure.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check found that XCP-ng pool does not have external authentication (AD/LDAP) configured to provide account enabling notifications to SA/ISSO. Without AD integration, local account enabling does not generate notifications. To remediate: (1) Configure external authentication: xe pool-enable-external-auth auth-type=AD service-name=domain.example.com; (2) Configure AD monitoring for account enable events (Event ID 4722); (3) Alternatively, configure auditd with mail forwarding for /etc/shadow changes combined with root mail alias to SA/ISSO. The ISSO must document notification procedures in the System Security Plan.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check verified that XCP-ng implements discretionary access controls through its RBAC system. Pool administrators can pass information (ISO images, virtual disks, network configurations) to any guest VM through shared storage repositories, and can share resources between VMs. The RBAC role hierarchy (pool-admin, pool-operator, vm-power-admin, vm-admin, vm-operator, read-only) controls which users can perform these operations. The xe CLI provides commands for managing VM resources, storage, and network assignments. This meets the VMM SRG requirement for discretionary access controls enabling information sharing.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check could not verify RBAC role configuration on XCP-ng. RBAC roles must be properly configured to allow administrators to pass information between VMs, users, and system components. To remediate: (1) Verify RBAC roles exist: xe role-list; (2) Configure external auth for centralized role management: xe pool-enable-external-auth auth-type=AD service-name=domain.example.com; (3) Assign appropriate roles to subjects: xe subject-role-add uuid=[subject-uuid] role-uuid=[role-uuid]; (4) Verify shared storage repositories are accessible to authorized administrators. The system administrator must ensure RBAC is properly configured for information sharing controls.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check verified that XCP-ng implements RBAC with a role hierarchy that allows pool administrators to grant privileges to other admins. Pool-admin role holders can assign any subordinate role (pool-operator, vm-power-admin, vm-admin, vm-operator, read-only) to other users via xe subject-role-add. This provides discretionary access control for privilege delegation, allowing administrators to grant their privileges to other VMM administrators as needed. The RBAC system ensures that privilege delegation is controlled and auditable.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check could not verify RBAC role configuration for privilege delegation. RBAC must be configured to allow administrators to grant their privileges to other administrators. To remediate: (1) Verify RBAC roles: xe role-list; (2) Enable external auth: xe pool-enable-external-auth auth-type=AD service-name=domain.example.com; (3) Assign roles: xe subject-role-add uuid=[subject-uuid] role-uuid=[role-uuid]. The pool-admin role provides full privilege delegation capability through the xe CLI.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check verified that XCP-ng RBAC allows pool administrators to change security attributes on users (xe subject-role-add/remove), guest VMs (xe vm-param-set for security tags, network assignments, storage access), and VMM components (xe pool-param-set, xe host-param-set). The pool-admin role has full control over all security-relevant attributes. Networks and storage repositories are manageable through the xe CLI, providing granular control over VM isolation and resource access. This meets the VMM SRG requirement for discretionary access control over security attributes.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check could not verify RBAC configuration for security attribute management. Configure XCP-ng RBAC to enable administrators to modify security attributes: (1) Verify roles: xe role-list; (2) Enable external auth for centralized management; (3) Assign pool-admin role to authorized security administrators. The pool-admin role provides full control over user roles, VM parameters, network assignments, and system configuration.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check verified that XCP-ng allows administrators to choose security attributes for newly created or revised guest VMs. During VM creation via xe vm-install or xe vm-create, administrators specify network assignments (VLAN isolation), storage repository placement, memory/CPU limits, boot parameters, and VM-level tags. VM templates provide standardized security baselines for new VMs. The RBAC system controls which users can create and modify VMs. This meets the VMM SRG requirement for discretionary access control over new VM security attribute selection.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check could not verify RBAC configuration for VM creation security attributes. Configure XCP-ng RBAC to control VM creation and attribute assignment: (1) Verify roles: xe role-list; (2) Create hardened VM templates with approved security configurations; (3) Assign vm-admin or higher role to administrators who need to create VMs. The xe vm-install and xe vm-create commands accept parameters for network, storage, and security-relevant attributes.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check verified that XCP-ng enforces privilege separation through multiple layers: (1) Xen hypervisor architecture isolates Dom0 (privileged) from DomU (unprivileged guest VMs), preventing guest VMs from executing host-level functions; (2) RBAC restricts XAPI operations based on role assignment, with read-only users unable to modify configurations; (3) Linux DAC and sudo on Dom0 restrict root-level operations to authorized administrators. Non-privileged users cannot disable, circumvent, or alter security safeguards. This multi-layered approach meets the VMM SRG requirement for preventing non-privileged privileged function execution.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check could not verify RBAC configuration for privilege separation. XCP-ng must be configured to prevent non-privileged users from executing privileged functions. To remediate: (1) Enable RBAC via external auth: xe pool-enable-external-auth auth-type=AD service-name=domain.example.com; (2) Assign restrictive roles (read-only, vm-operator) to non-privileged users; (3) Restrict Dom0 SSH access to authorized administrators only via /etc/ssh/sshd_config AllowUsers directive; (4) Configure sudo to limit root access. Verify RBAC roles are properly assigned with: xe subject-list.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check verified that XCP-ng/Xen prevents software from executing at higher privilege levels through hardware-enforced ring architecture. The Xen hypervisor runs at Ring -1 (most privileged), the Dom0 kernel at Ring 0 (hardware access mediated by Xen), and user space/guest VMs at Ring 3 (least privileged). Guest VMs cannot access Ring 0 or Ring -1 — all hardware access is mediated through Xen hypercalls. XAPI RBAC further restricts management operations based on assigned role. SUID binary analysis and mount point nosuid checks confirm Dom0 privilege escalation controls. This hardware-enforced privilege separation meets the VMM SRG requirement.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: This finding should not normally occur for XCP-ng, as the Xen hypervisor architecture inherently enforces privilege level separation through hardware virtualization extensions (VT-x/AMD-V). If this finding is Open, investigate whether the Xen hypervisor is properly loaded and hardware virtualization is enabled in BIOS/UEFI. Verify with: xl info | grep virt_caps. Ensure Dom0 SUID binaries are minimized and /tmp is mounted with nosuid. The Xen ring architecture (-1/0/3) provides the fundamental privilege separation that prevents software from escalating privileges.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check verified that auditd is active and configured to audit privileged function execution on XCP-ng. Audit rules monitor execve system calls for privilege escalation and/or specifically track privileged commands (sudo, su). Additionally, XAPI logs all hypervisor management operations in /var/log/xensource.log, providing application-level audit of privileged VMM functions including VM lifecycle, storage operations, and configuration changes. This dual-layer approach (Linux auditd + XAPI management logging) meets the VMM SRG requirement for auditing privileged function execution.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check found that auditd is not properly configured to audit privileged function execution. To remediate: (1) Enable auditd: systemctl enable --now auditd; (2) Add privileged function audit rules: auditctl -a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S execve -C uid!=euid -F euid=0 -k privileged; auditctl -w /usr/bin/sudo -p x -k privileged-sudo; auditctl -w /bin/su -p x -k privileged-su; (3) Make persistent in /etc/audit/rules.d/privileged.rules; (4) Reload: augenrules --load. These rules capture all privilege escalation events for security audit review.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check verified that XCP-ng is configured with pam_faillock for account lockout policy. The PAM configuration in /etc/pam.d/system-auth includes pam_faillock with deny=3 (lock after 3 failures), fail_interval=900 (15-minute window), and unlock_time=0 (administrator-only unlock). This configuration locks accounts after three unsuccessful logon attempts within 15 minutes and requires an administrator to release the locked account using faillock --user [username] --reset. This meets the DoD requirement for account lockout per the VMM SRG.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check found that account lockout is not properly configured on XCP-ng to meet DoD requirements. The pam_faillock module must be configured with deny <= 3, fail_interval >= 900, and unlock_time = 0. To remediate, add to /etc/pam.d/system-auth and /etc/pam.d/password-auth: (1) In auth section, before pam_unix: auth required pam_faillock.so preauth silent deny=3 fail_interval=900 unlock_time=0; (2) After pam_unix: auth required pam_faillock.so authfail deny=3 fail_interval=900 unlock_time=0; (3) In account section: account required pam_faillock.so. Verify with: faillock --user root. SSH MaxAuthTries should also be set to 3 or fewer in /etc/ssh/sshd_config.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check verified that XCP-ng provides audit configuration change capability through auditctl and the audit rules framework. Authorized administrators (SA/ISSO) can modify audit rules in near real-time using auditctl -a/-d (add/delete rules), auditctl -D (delete all rules), or by editing /etc/audit/rules.d/ and running augenrules --load. Changes take effect immediately without service restart. The auditd service is active and the auditctl tool is available, providing full capability to adjust auditing based on selectable event criteria. This meets the VMM SRG requirement for near real-time audit configuration changes.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check found that auditd is not properly configured to allow near real-time audit configuration changes. To remediate: (1) Install audit package: yum install audit; (2) Enable auditd: systemctl enable --now auditd; (3) Verify auditctl is available: which auditctl; (4) Ensure SA/ISSO accounts have sudo access to auditctl for adding, removing, and modifying audit rules; (5) Create audit rules directory: mkdir -p /etc/audit/rules.d; (6) Test rule changes: auditctl -w /tmp -p wa -k test-rule, then verify with auditctl -l. Audit configuration must be changeable in near real-time to respond to security events.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated STIG check verified that the auditd service is active and audit record storage is configured with sufficient capacity. The check examines auditd.conf settings including max_log_file (individual log size in MB), num_logs (number of rotated logs), and max_log_file_action (rotation behavior). The total audit storage capacity is calculated as max_log_file multiplied by num_logs. The check also reports the available disk space on the /var/log/audit partition. XCP-ng hypervisors typically generate moderate audit volume, and the configured capacity provides sufficient storage for at least one week of audit records. The auditd log rotation mechanism (max_log_file_action) ensures older logs are managed according to policy. This satisfies the NIST AU-4 requirement for adequate audit storage capacity when records are not immediately forwarded to a central facility.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated STIG check found that audit record storage capacity is insufficient or auditd is not active. The check examines auditd.conf for max_log_file, num_logs, and max_log_file_action settings and calculates total capacity. A minimum of 40 MB total capacity is required for the automated check to pass, though actual requirements depend on audit volume. To remediate: (1) Enable auditd if not active: systemctl enable --now auditd. (2) Configure adequate storage in /etc/audit/auditd.conf: set max_log_file to at least 50 MB, num_logs to at least 10, and max_log_file_action to ROTATE. (3) Ensure the /var/log/audit partition has sufficient free space. (4) Monitor audit log growth over one week to calibrate storage requirements. The system administrator should work with the ISSO to determine appropriate retention capacity based on the organization's audit policy and the hypervisor's typical audit event volume.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated STIG check verified that audit records are configured for off-loading to a remote system. The check examines rsyslog configuration for remote forwarding targets (using @@ for TCP or @ for UDP syslog forwarding) and the audisp au-remote plugin for dedicated audit record forwarding. At least one remote log forwarding mechanism was found to be active, ensuring audit records are sent to a different system than the XCP-ng hypervisor being audited. This provides defense-in-depth by maintaining audit records on a separate system where they cannot be tampered with by a compromised hypervisor. The remote log server should be configured to receive and retain these records per organizational audit retention policy. This satisfies the NIST AU-4(1) requirement for off-loading audit records to a separate system.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated STIG check found that no remote audit off-loading is configured. Neither rsyslog remote forwarding nor the audisp au-remote plugin is active. To remediate: (1) Configure rsyslog for remote forwarding by adding a line such as '*.* @@logserver.domain:514' to /etc/rsyslog.conf (use @@ for TCP, @ for UDP). (2) Alternatively, install audispd-plugins (yum install audispd-plugins), enable the au-remote plugin in /etc/audisp/plugins.d/au-remote.conf (set active = yes), and configure the remote server in /etc/audisp/audisp-remote.conf. (3) Restart the appropriate service: systemctl restart rsyslog or systemctl restart auditd. The system administrator must ensure audit records are forwarded to a centralized log management system that is separate from the XCP-ng hypervisor, as required by NIST AU-4(1) and organizational audit policy.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated STIG check verified that auditd is configured with space_left and space_left_action settings to warn when audit storage reaches the threshold. The space_left parameter defines the remaining disk space (in MB) that triggers the warning, and space_left_action is set to an acceptable notification action (email, exec, or syslog). The space_left value should represent approximately 25% of total audit partition capacity to trigger at the 75% usage mark. This configuration ensures that the SA and ISSO receive immediate warning when audit record storage approaches capacity, allowing proactive intervention before audit records are lost. The admin_space_left and admin_space_left_action settings provide an additional critical-level warning. This satisfies NIST AU-5(1) requirements for storage capacity threshold notification.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated STIG check found that auditd is not properly configured to warn when audit storage reaches 75% capacity. Either space_left is not configured or space_left_action is set to an unacceptable value (ignore or suspend). To remediate: (1) Calculate 25% of the /var/log/audit partition size. (2) Set space_left to that value in /etc/audit/auditd.conf. (3) Set space_left_action to 'email' (requires configured mail system) or 'exec' (to run a custom notification script). (4) Optionally set admin_space_left to a smaller value and admin_space_left_action to 'halt' or 'single' for critical-level warnings. (5) Restart auditd: systemctl restart auditd. The system administrator must work with the ISSO to ensure notification reaches designated personnel when storage reaches the 75% threshold.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated STIG check verified that auditd is configured with appropriate failure event actions. The check examines disk_full_action, disk_error_action, and admin_space_left_action in /etc/audit/auditd.conf. All three are set to acceptable alert actions (email, exec, syslog, halt, or single) that provide immediate notification or system protection when audit failures occur. disk_full_action responds when the audit partition is full, disk_error_action responds to disk I/O errors affecting audit logging, and admin_space_left_action provides critical-level space warnings. These actions ensure the SA and ISSO are immediately alerted to audit failure events that could result in lost audit records, satisfying NIST AU-5 requirements for real-time audit failure alerting.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated STIG check found that auditd is not fully configured for real-time audit failure alerts. One or more failure event actions (disk_full_action, disk_error_action, admin_space_left_action) are either not configured or set to unacceptable values (ignore or suspend). To remediate, set the following in /etc/audit/auditd.conf: disk_full_action = halt (stops system if audit disk is full, preventing unaudited operations), disk_error_action = halt (stops system on audit I/O errors), admin_space_left_action = email or exec (sends notification when critically low on space). After making changes, restart auditd: systemctl restart auditd. For email alerts, ensure a mail transport agent is configured. For exec alerts, create a notification script that alerts SA/ISSO via the organization's alerting system.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated STIG check verified that ausearch and aureport are installed, providing audit reduction capability for on-demand review and analysis. ausearch enables filtering audit records by time range (-ts/-te), user (-ua), event type (-m), key (-k), process ID (-p), and other criteria. aureport generates summary reports across multiple audit event categories. These tools are part of the standard Linux audit package and operate on the binary audit log format stored in /var/log/audit/. Together they provide comprehensive audit reduction capability allowing authorized administrators to perform targeted, on-demand review and analysis of audit records without requiring external tools. This satisfies NIST AU-7 requirements for audit reduction capability.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated STIG check found that audit reduction tools (ausearch and/or aureport) are not installed. These tools are essential for on-demand review and analysis of audit records. To remediate: install the audit package using yum install audit. This provides ausearch for targeted filtering and searching of audit records and aureport for generating summary analysis reports. After installation, verify the tools are available: which ausearch aureport. The system administrator should ensure auditd is also active (systemctl enable --now auditd) to generate the audit records that these tools analyze. Without audit reduction capability, administrators cannot efficiently review the volume of audit data generated by the hypervisor.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated STIG check verified that ausearch and aureport are installed, providing audit reduction capability for after-the-fact investigation of security incidents. ausearch supports forensic-grade filtering including time-range correlation (-ts/-te for incident timeframes), user tracking (-ua/-ui), process analysis (-p), syscall investigation (-sc), and keyword-based search (-k). Multiple archived audit log files are retained, enabling historical investigation. aureport provides incident-focused summary reports including authentication events, anomalies, and failed actions. These capabilities enable incident responders to reconstruct event timelines, identify affected users and processes, and correlate audit evidence across the hypervisor's audit trail. This satisfies NIST AU-7 requirements for after-the-fact investigation support.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated STIG check found that audit reduction tools for after-the-fact investigations are not installed. Without ausearch and aureport, incident responders cannot efficiently analyze audit records to investigate security incidents. To remediate: install the audit package (yum install audit) and ensure auditd is running (systemctl enable --now auditd). Configure adequate audit log retention in /etc/audit/auditd.conf (increase num_logs and max_log_file) to ensure sufficient historical data for investigations. The system administrator should also configure audit rules to capture security-relevant events (privilege escalation, authentication, file access) that are typically needed during incident investigations.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated STIG check verified that aureport is installed, providing report generation capability for on-demand audit review and analysis. aureport generates structured reports across multiple audit event categories: -l (logins), -f (file access), -x (executables), -u (users), -p (processes), -s (syscalls), -a (anomaly events), -c (configuration changes). Reports can be filtered by time range (-ts/-te) and combined with --summary for statistical overviews. The tool operates on the binary audit log format and produces human-readable output suitable for on-demand review by SA/ISSO personnel. This satisfies NIST AU-7 requirements for report generation capability supporting on-demand audit review and analysis.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated STIG check found that report generation tools are not installed. aureport is required for generating structured audit reports for on-demand review. To remediate: install the audit package (yum install audit) which provides aureport along with ausearch and other audit tools. After installation, verify: which aureport. Ensure auditd is active to generate the audit records that aureport analyzes. The system administrator should familiarize themselves with aureport's report categories (logins, files, users, anomalies, etc.) and time-range filtering to support on-demand reporting requirements from the ISSO or auditors.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated STIG check verified that aureport and ausearch are installed along with standard system reporting tools (last, lastlog). aureport supports multiple report types that fulfill various on-demand reporting requirements: login reports (-l), file access reports (-f), executable reports (-x), user activity reports (-u), process reports (-p), syscall reports (-s), anomaly reports (-a), modification reports (-m), and statistical summaries (--summary). The last and lastlog commands provide additional login history reporting. These tools collectively enable the generation of customizable, on-demand audit reports that satisfy organizational reporting requirements for STIG compliance, incident response, and routine security monitoring.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated STIG check found that report generation tools are not installed. aureport is required for generating customizable audit reports. To remediate: install the audit package (yum install audit) to provide aureport and ausearch. Ensure auditd is active (systemctl enable --now auditd). After installation, administrators can generate on-demand reports using aureport with category flags (-l, -f, -x, -u, -p, -s, -a, -m) and time-range filtering (-ts/-te). The system administrator should establish standard operating procedures for generating periodic and on-demand audit reports to meet organizational reporting requirements.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated STIG check verified that aureport and ausearch are installed, providing report generation capability for after-the-fact investigation of security incidents. aureport generates incident-focused reports including anomaly events (-a), failed actions (--failed), authentication events (-au), login attempts (--login), and account modifications (-m). Combined with ausearch time-range filtering, incident responders can generate targeted forensic reports across audit log history. Xen hypervisor logs (/var/log/xen/) provide additional hypervisor-specific event data. This multi-source report generation capability enables comprehensive after-the-fact investigation of security incidents affecting the hypervisor, including VM-level events, management API operations, and system-level security events.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated STIG check found that report generation tools for incident investigation are not installed. Install the audit package (yum install audit) to provide aureport for generating incident-focused reports and ausearch for forensic data correlation. Ensure auditd is active and configure adequate log retention for historical investigation. The system administrator should ensure audit rules capture security-relevant events and that incident responders have appropriate access to audit tools and log files for generating investigation reports.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated STIG check verified that ausearch operates in read-only mode against audit log files. ausearch filters and displays audit records without modifying the original audit.log files in /var/log/audit/. The Linux audit subsystem writes records sequentially with kernel-generated timestamps, and ausearch preserves this chronological time ordering in its output. The audit log files are protected by restrictive file permissions (typically 600 root:root) ensuring only the audit daemon can write to them. ausearch is a standard component of the Linux Audit Framework maintained by Red Hat and is designed specifically for non-destructive read-only access to audit records. This architecture ensures original content and time ordering are never altered during audit reduction operations.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated STIG check found that ausearch is not installed. Install the audit package (yum install audit) to provide ausearch, which operates in read-only mode and preserves original content and time ordering of audit records during reduction operations. The standard Linux audit tools are designed to never modify the original audit log data.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated STIG check verified that aureport operates in read-only mode against audit log files. aureport reads audit records and generates summary reports without modifying the original audit.log content in /var/log/audit/. Reports maintain chronological time ordering based on kernel-generated epoch timestamps. The audit log files are protected by restrictive file permissions ensuring only the audit daemon can write to them. aureport is a standard component of the Linux Audit Framework and is designed specifically for non-destructive report generation from binary audit logs. This ensures original content and time ordering of audit records are preserved during all report generation operations.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated STIG check found that aureport is not installed. Install the audit package (yum install audit) to provide aureport, which operates in read-only mode and preserves original content and time ordering of audit records when generating reports. The standard Linux audit reporting tools are designed to never modify the original audit log data.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated STIG check verified that NTP time synchronization is active (via chronyd or ntpd) with authoritative time sources configured. The check examines service status, configured time servers from chrony.conf or ntp.conf, and current synchronization status. NTP clients poll time servers at intervals ranging from 64 seconds (minpoll 6) to 1024 seconds (maxpoll 10) by default, which far exceeds the 24-hour comparison requirement. Chrony sources and tracking information confirm active synchronization with the configured authoritative time servers. This ensures the XCP-ng hypervisor's internal clock is continuously compared with authoritative time sources well within the required 24-hour interval, satisfying NIST AU-8(1)(a) requirements.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated STIG check found that NTP time synchronization is not properly configured. Either no NTP service (chronyd or ntpd) is active, or no authoritative time sources are configured. To remediate: (1) Enable chronyd: systemctl enable --now chronyd. (2) Configure authoritative time sources in /etc/chrony.conf, such as DoD NTP servers (e.g., server ntp.mil iburst) or organizational time servers. (3) Restart chronyd: systemctl restart chronyd. (4) Verify synchronization: chronyc sources. The system administrator must configure at least one authoritative time source to ensure the internal clock is compared at least every 24 hours as required by NIST AU-8(1)(a).
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated STIG check verified that NTP time synchronization is active via chronyd or ntpd. The check examines service status, chrony.conf or ntp.conf for the makestep directive, and current time offset from the authoritative source. chronyd uses the makestep directive to enable immediate clock stepping for offsets larger than the configured threshold and continuous slewing for smaller offsets. Both mechanisms ensure the system clock is automatically synchronized when the time difference exceeds one second. The current system time offset and root delay are reported to confirm active synchronization. This satisfies NIST AU-8(1)(b) requirements for automatic clock synchronization when time difference exceeds one second.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated STIG check found that no NTP time synchronization service is active. Without NTP, the system clock will drift and cannot be automatically corrected when the difference exceeds one second. To remediate: (1) Enable chronyd: systemctl enable --now chronyd. (2) Configure the makestep directive in /etc/chrony.conf (e.g., makestep 1 3) to allow clock stepping for offsets greater than 1 second during the first 3 updates. (3) Configure authoritative time sources. (4) Restart chronyd and verify: chronyc tracking. This ensures compliance with NIST AU-8(1)(b) automatic synchronization requirements.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated STIG check verified that auditd is active and recording timestamps with sub-second (millisecond) precision, exceeding the one-second granularity requirement. The Linux Audit Framework records each audit event with a timestamp in epoch format (msg=audit(EPOCH.MILLISECONDS:SERIAL)), where the decimal portion provides millisecond-level precision. This is a built-in capability of the kernel audit subsystem that cannot be configured to a lower granularity. A sample audit record is displayed to demonstrate the timestamp format. The log_format setting confirms the audit log output format. This inherent sub-second precision satisfies the NIST AU-8(2) requirement for one-second minimum timestamp granularity.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated STIG check found that auditd is not active. Without the audit service running, no audit records with timestamps are being generated. Enable the audit service: systemctl enable --now auditd. The Linux audit framework inherently provides sub-second (millisecond) timestamp precision when active, exceeding the one-second granularity requirement. No additional configuration is needed for timestamp precision once auditd is running.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated STIG check verified that auditd is active and records timestamps in epoch format (seconds since 1970-01-01 00:00:00 UTC). Epoch timestamps are inherently in UTC and directly mappable to Coordinated Universal Time regardless of the system's configured local timezone. The check displays the system timezone configuration and confirms auditd is generating records. Tools such as ausearch and aureport can convert epoch timestamps to human-readable UTC or local time representations. This architecture ensures all audit records can be correlated across systems using a common UTC time reference, satisfying NIST AU-8(2) requirements for UTC-mappable audit timestamps.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated STIG check found that auditd is not active. Without the audit service running, no audit records with UTC-mappable timestamps are being generated. Enable the audit service: systemctl enable --now auditd. The Linux audit framework records timestamps in UTC-based epoch format, which satisfies the UTC/GMT mapping requirement. Optionally, set the system timezone to UTC (timedatectl set-timezone UTC) for consistency with audit timestamps.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated STIG check reports audit log file permissions, directory ownership, audit rule immutability status, and file watches. Dual authorization for movement/deletion of audit information requires organizational policy controls beyond what XCP-ng can enforce technically through its native capabilities. While audit log files are restricted to root ownership with restrictive permissions, and audit rules can be made immutable (-e 2), the dual authorization requirement (two separate individuals must independently authorize) must be enforced through organizational procedures. Recommended organizational controls include: requiring ISSO approval before SA can delete or move audit logs via a formal change request process, implementing ticket-based change control for audit data operations, maintaining a log of all audit data movement and deletion activities, and configuring sudo to require a separate authorization for audit-related commands.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated STIG check verified that XCP-ng enforces privileged access for software and VM installation through multiple mechanisms. RPM/YUM package installation requires root privileges as the binaries are owned by root with standard permissions. XAPI RBAC restricts VM creation and management operations to pool-admin and pool-operator roles. Dom0 SSH access is limited to authorized administrators. The check examines yum and rpm binary permissions, RBAC role availability, configured RBAC subjects, and sudo configuration. Non-privileged users cannot install software packages or create guest VMs without explicit elevated privileges. This multi-layer privilege enforcement satisfies NIST CM-11(2) requirements for prohibiting user software installation without explicit privileged status.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated STIG check found potential weaknesses in software installation privilege controls. Review and tighten access controls: (1) Ensure yum/rpm binaries have restrictive permissions (root ownership, no world-execute on management scripts). (2) Configure XAPI RBAC with explicit role assignments via xe subject-add. (3) Restrict sudo access to authorized administrators only. (4) Limit SSH access to Dom0 using AllowUsers/AllowGroups in sshd_config. Non-privileged users must not be able to install software or create VMs without explicit administrative authorization.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated STIG check verified that file integrity monitoring (AIDE) is installed and configured to detect unauthorized baseline configuration changes. AIDE compares current file states against a known-good baseline database and reports modifications, additions, and deletions. The check examines AIDE binary availability, configuration file presence, audit rules for configuration file watches, and scheduled AIDE scanning via cron jobs. When AIDE detects unauthorized changes, it generates reports that can be configured to notify SA/ISSO personnel via email or integration with centralized monitoring systems. This satisfies NIST CM-6(2) requirements for baseline configuration change notification.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated STIG check found that no file integrity monitoring tool is configured to detect and notify on unauthorized baseline changes. To remediate: (1) Install AIDE: yum install aide. (2) Configure /etc/aide.conf with baseline paths including /etc/xensource/, /etc/xapi.conf, /etc/audit/, /etc/ssh/sshd_config, and other critical configuration files. (3) Initialize the baseline database: aide --init, then cp /var/lib/aide/aide.db.new.gz /var/lib/aide/aide.db.gz. (4) Configure a daily cron job: echo '0 5 * * * root /usr/sbin/aide --check | mail -s "AIDE Report" isso@domain' > /etc/cron.d/aide-check. (5) After any authorized change, update the baseline: aide --update. The system administrator must ensure AIDE reports are reviewed by designated personnel (SA/ISSO).
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated STIG check verified that XCP-ng enforces access restrictions for system changes through multiple layers. XAPI RBAC provides a role hierarchy (pool-admin, pool-operator, vm-power-admin, vm-admin, vm-operator, read-only) that restricts management API operations to authorized roles. Linux DAC file permissions restrict access to critical configuration files (/etc/xapi.conf, /etc/xensource/). sudo controls limit which users can execute privileged commands. SSH access to Dom0 is restricted to authorized administrators. Only the pool-admin role can modify pool-level configurations and system settings. The check reports RBAC role availability, configured subjects, sudoers configuration, and file permissions on critical hypervisor configuration files. This multi-layer access control satisfies NIST CM-5 requirements.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated STIG check identified potential gaps in access restriction enforcement. Review and strengthen controls: (1) Configure XAPI RBAC with AD/LDAP integration (xe pool-enable-external-auth) and assign explicit roles to subjects. (2) Restrict file permissions on critical configs: chmod 600 /etc/xapi.conf, chown root:root. (3) Harden sudo configuration to limit privileged command access. (4) Add AllowUsers/AllowGroups to sshd_config to restrict Dom0 SSH access. Only authorized administrators should be able to modify system configurations.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated STIG check verified that auditd is active and configured to audit access restriction enforcement actions. The check examines audit rules for privilege escalation monitoring (sudo/su), permission change tracking (chmod/chown/setxattr), and access denial recording (EPERM/EACCES). The /var/log/secure log records authentication and authorization events including failed access attempts. XAPI logs (xensource.log) record API-level access restriction enforcement for RBAC-controlled operations. These combined logging mechanisms provide a comprehensive audit trail of all access restriction enforcement actions across the hypervisor's management plane, OS layer, and XAPI management API. This satisfies NIST CM-5(1) requirements for auditing enforcement actions.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated STIG check found that auditd is not active. Enable auditd to audit access restriction enforcement: systemctl enable --now auditd. Configure audit rules: (1) sudo monitoring: -w /etc/sudoers -p wa -k actions. (2) su monitoring: -w /usr/bin/su -p x -k priv_esc. (3) Permission changes: -a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S chmod,chown,setxattr -k perm_mod. (4) Access denials: -a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S open -F exit=-EACCES -k access. Add rules to /etc/audit/rules.d/ and reload: augenrules --load. These rules ensure all enforcement actions are audited per NIST CM-5(1).
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated STIG check verified that RPM/YUM GPG signature verification is enabled globally (gpgcheck=1 in /etc/yum.conf) and no repositories have GPG checking disabled. All packages must be signed with an approved GPG key before installation. XCP-ng packages are signed with the Vates/XCP-ng GPG key, and the check confirms installed GPG public keys. The check examines the global yum.conf gpgcheck setting, localpkg_gpgcheck setting, individual repository configurations, and counts repos with gpgcheck disabled. This ensures patches, drivers, and VMM components are cryptographically verified before installation, satisfying NIST CM-14 requirements for digital signature verification of software installations.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated STIG check found that GPG signature verification is not fully enforced for package installation. Either gpgcheck is not set to 1 in /etc/yum.conf or one or more repository configurations have gpgcheck=0. To remediate: (1) Set gpgcheck=1 in /etc/yum.conf under the [main] section. (2) For each .repo file in /etc/yum.repos.d/ that has gpgcheck=0, change it to gpgcheck=1 and ensure the gpgkey URL points to the correct GPG public key. (3) Import the XCP-ng GPG key if not already: rpm --import https://xcp-ng.org/RPM-GPG-KEY-xcpng. All package installations must be verified against approved digital signatures to prevent installation of unauthorized or tampered software.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated STIG check verified that XCP-ng does not deploy service or helper VMs as part of its core hypervisor function. Unlike some hypervisors (e.g., VMware vCLS), XCP-ng uses Dom0 (the control domain) for all management operations rather than dedicated service VMs. The check enumerates all guest VMs (non-template, non-control-domain) with their power states and reports the total count. All running VMs are explicitly created by administrators. XAPI RBAC controls restrict VM creation and management to authorized roles (pool-admin, pool-operator). The check also identifies any known service VMs such as XOA Proxy. Administrators should periodically review the VM inventory to ensure no unnecessary service or helper VMs are present beyond those required for authorized management functions.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated STIG check found potential concerns with service or helper VMs. Review the VM inventory displayed in the finding details and remove any VMs that are not required to support proper VMM function. Use xe vm-list to enumerate all VMs, identify their purpose, and remove unauthorized service/helper VMs with xe vm-destroy. Document the business justification for any service VMs that must remain (e.g., XOA Proxy for remote backup). Ensure RBAC is configured to prevent unauthorized VM creation.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated STIG check verified that XCP-ng provides controls to prevent inappropriate use of redundant guest VMs. XAPI RBAC restricts VM creation to pool-admin and pool-operator roles, preventing unauthorized VM proliferation. The check reports the complete VM inventory including name, power state, total count, halted (stopped) VMs, and snapshot count. The hypervisor maintains comprehensive lifecycle tracking for all VMs. Administrators should review: (1) halted VMs for unnecessary dormant instances that consume storage, (2) snapshots for stale checkpoint data, and (3) naming conventions to identify potential duplicates. VM lifecycle management is enforced through RBAC access controls ensuring only authorized administrators can create or clone VMs.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated STIG check identified potential redundant guest VM concerns. Review the VM inventory and identify any redundant or duplicate VMs. Remove unnecessary halted VMs: xe vm-destroy uuid=VM_UUID. Clean up stale snapshots: xe snapshot-destroy uuid=SNAP_UUID. Implement naming conventions to prevent confusion between production and test VMs. Configure RBAC to restrict VM creation to authorized administrators only. Document the purpose of each VM and establish periodic review procedures to identify and remove redundant instances.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated STIG check verified that XCP-ng employs a deny-all, permit-by-exception policy for execution through multiple mechanisms. XAPI RBAC denies all management operations by default; users must be explicitly assigned a role to execute any VM or management operation. The check examines iptables default chain policies, RBAC role configuration, external authentication type, and SSH access restrictions. The read-only role provides the minimum baseline, and all additional privileges must be explicitly granted. Dom0 SSH access is restricted to authorized accounts. iptables firewall policies control network-level access with configurable default policies. Only root and authorized RBAC subjects can start, stop, or create VMs. This multi-layer deny-all approach satisfies NIST CM-7(5) requirements for deny-all, permit-by-exception execution policies.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated STIG check identified potential gaps in the deny-all, permit-by-exception execution policy. Review and strengthen: (1) Configure XAPI RBAC with AD/LDAP integration and assign explicit, least-privilege roles. (2) Set iptables default policies to DROP for INPUT and FORWARD chains: iptables -P INPUT DROP, iptables -P FORWARD DROP. (3) Add explicit ACCEPT rules only for required services (SSH, XAPI). (4) Restrict SSH access using AllowUsers/AllowGroups in sshd_config. (5) Remove any users from roles higher than needed (principle of least privilege). The default posture must be deny-all with explicit exceptions for authorized operations only.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check verified that multifactor authentication infrastructure is configured for remote access to privileged accounts on XCP-ng. The check examines external authentication integration (AD/LDAP), SSH authentication methods (public key authentication), and PAM MFA modules (pam_google_authenticator, pam_oath, pam_duo, pam_pkcs11). When external authentication or PAM MFA modules are detected, the system provides MFA capability where one factor (knowledge/password) is supplemented by a separate device factor (hardware token, smart card, or mobile authenticator). The ISSO/ISSM should verify that the MFA factor is provided by a device physically separate from the system being accessed, per NIST SP 800-63B requirements.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check found no multifactor authentication infrastructure for remote access to privileged accounts on XCP-ng. No external authentication (AD/LDAP), no PAM MFA modules (pam_google_authenticator, pam_oath, pam_duo), and no smart card modules (pam_pkcs11) were detected. DoD requires MFA for all remote privileged access per NIST SP 800-53 IA-2(1). To remediate: (1) Configure AD/LDAP external authentication with MFA enforcement via xe pool-join-external-auth, or (2) Install and configure PAM-based MFA such as Google Authenticator (yum install google-authenticator-libpam) or Duo Security, or (3) Deploy smart card authentication via pam_pkcs11 with DoD CAC/PIV cards. The system administrator must ensure the second authentication factor is provided by a device separate from the system gaining access.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check verified that PIV credential acceptance infrastructure is configured on XCP-ng. The check examines pam_pkcs11 package installation, opensc smart card middleware, pcsc-lite daemon, PAM PKCS11 configuration, SSSD smart card support, and external authentication integration. PIV credential acceptance is confirmed when pam_pkcs11 is configured in PAM or when external authentication (AD/LDAP with PKINIT) provides PIV acceptance through the identity provider. Active Directory with PKINIT enables Kerberos smart card logon, satisfying the PIV acceptance requirement at the enterprise level.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check found no PIV credential acceptance infrastructure on XCP-ng. The pam_pkcs11 package is not installed, no smart card PAM configuration exists, and no external authentication is configured. DoD requires PIV credential acceptance per HSPD-12 and FIPS 201. To remediate: Install pam_pkcs11 (yum install pam_pkcs11), opensc (yum install opensc), and pcsc-lite (yum install pcsc-lite). Configure PAM for smart card authentication in /etc/pam.d/system-auth. Alternatively, integrate with Active Directory that supports PIV via PKINIT for Kerberos smart card logon. The ISSO/ISSM should coordinate with the organization PKI team for DoD CA certificate installation.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check verified that electronic PIV credential verification is configured on XCP-ng. The check examines pam_pkcs11 configuration for certificate verification, CRL/OCSP checking settings, CA trust anchors, OpenSSL verification configuration, and external authentication integration. Electronic verification confirms PIV certificate validity through certificate chain validation, revocation checking (CRL or OCSP), and trust anchor verification against DoD Root CAs. When external authentication is configured, PIV electronic verification is delegated to the identity provider which performs certificate validation during smart card logon.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check found no PIV electronic verification infrastructure on XCP-ng. No pam_pkcs11 configuration exists for certificate verification and no external authentication is configured. To remediate: Configure pam_pkcs11 with CRL and OCSP checking in /etc/pam_pkcs11/pam_pkcs11.conf. Install DoD Root CA certificates in /etc/pki/ca-trust/source/anchors/ and run update-ca-trust. Configure CRL download schedules and OCSP responder URLs for real-time certificate validation. Alternatively, integrate with AD/LDAP that performs electronic PIV credential validation during authentication.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check verified that peripheral authentication controls are in place on XCP-ng. The check examines USB device authorization policy, udev rules, USBGuard package and service status, and Xen PCI passthrough controls. USBGuard provides device whitelisting for USB peripherals, or USB default authorization is set to deny. The Xen hypervisor architecture ensures only Dom0 has direct peripheral access; guest VMs cannot directly access peripherals without explicit PCI passthrough configuration by authorized pool-admin RBAC role holders.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check found no peripheral authentication enforcement beyond Xen default isolation on XCP-ng. USBGuard is not installed and USB default authorization allows all devices. To remediate: Install USBGuard (yum install usbguard) and configure device whitelisting in /etc/usbguard/rules.conf. Alternatively, set USB default authorization to deny: echo 0 > /sys/bus/usb/devices/usb*/authorized_default and persist via udev rules. Configure udev rules in /etc/udev/rules.d/ for specific device authentication requirements.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check verified bidirectional cryptographic authentication on XCP-ng. SSH host keys are present for server authentication and public key or password authentication verifies the client. XAPI uses TLS certificates for encrypted management connections. Both protocols provide cryptographically-based mutual authentication between endpoints: SSH uses Diffie-Hellman key exchange for server identity verification and the client authenticates via public key or password; XAPI HTTPS uses X.509 certificates for server authentication and session tokens for client authentication.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check found incomplete bidirectional cryptographic authentication on XCP-ng. SSH host keys or XAPI TLS certificate may be missing. To remediate: Ensure SSH host keys exist in /etc/ssh/ (ssh-keygen -A if missing). Verify XAPI TLS certificate at /etc/xensource/xapi-ssl.pem is valid. Configure SSH for public key authentication (PubkeyAuthentication yes). For enhanced mutual TLS, configure stunnel with client certificate verification (verify = 2 in stunnel.conf).
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check verified that cached authenticators are properly controlled on XCP-ng. The check examines SSSD offline credential cache settings, sudo timestamp timeout, XAPI session timeout, and Kerberos ticket lifetime. XCP-ng without SSSD uses direct PAM authentication with no credential caching. SSH requires fresh authentication for every connection. Sudo credential caching defaults to 5 minutes (well under 1 day). When SSSD is configured, offline_credentials_expiration is set to 1 day or less, complying with the requirement to prohibit cached authenticators after one day.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check found that SSSD offline credential cache exceeds 1 day on XCP-ng. To remediate: Set offline_credentials_expiration = 1 in /etc/sssd/sssd.conf under the [pam] section to limit cached credentials to 1 day. Restart SSSD service after changes: systemctl restart sssd. Additionally verify sudo timestamp_timeout is not set excessively high in /etc/sudoers.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check verified that local PKI revocation data cache is available on XCP-ng. Local CRL files and/or OCSP configuration provide offline certificate revocation checking. This supports path discovery and validation when network access to revocation servers is unavailable, ensuring PKI-based authentication can still verify certificate validity against locally cached revocation data per NIST SP 800-53 IA-5(2).
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check found no local PKI revocation data cache on XCP-ng. No CRL files exist and no OCSP configuration was found. To remediate: Download DoD CRL files to /etc/pki/ or /etc/pam_pkcs11/crls/. Configure automated CRL refresh via cron. Enable OCSP with local response caching in OpenSSL configuration. Install certutil for certificate management. This ensures certificate validation continues during network outages.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check verified that FIPS 140-2 mode is enabled at the kernel level on XCP-ng. All cryptographic operations use FIPS-validated modules, meeting the requirement for NSA-approved cryptography to protect classified information. SSH ciphers, MACs, and key exchange algorithms are restricted to FIPS-approved algorithms. OpenSSL operates in FIPS mode for all TLS operations.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check found that FIPS mode is not enabled on XCP-ng. NSA-approved cryptography requires FIPS 140-2 validated cryptographic modules per NIST SP 800-53 SC-13. To remediate: Install dracut-fips (yum install dracut-fips), add fips=1 to kernel boot parameters in /boot/grub2/grub.cfg, and regenerate initramfs (dracut -f). Reboot and verify FIPS mode with cat /proc/sys/crypto/fips_enabled. Note: XCP-ng/CentOS 7 FIPS mode may have compatibility limitations with some hypervisor functions. Test thoroughly before production deployment.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check verified that a DNSSEC-capable resolver is installed on XCP-ng. A DNSSEC-validating resolver (unbound or bind) provides data origin authentication verification for DNS responses from authoritative sources through DNSSEC chain of trust validation, RRSIG signature verification, and DNSKEY authentication.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check found no DNSSEC-validating resolver on XCP-ng. To remediate: Install unbound (yum install unbound), configure dnssec-validation in /etc/unbound/unbound.conf with auto-trust-anchor-file for root trust anchor, and point /etc/resolv.conf to the local resolver (nameserver 127.0.0.1). Enable and start the service: systemctl enable --now unbound.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check verified that a DNSSEC-capable resolver is available on XCP-ng. The system can request data integrity verification (RRSIG/DNSKEY) on DNS responses from authoritative sources via DNSSEC protocol, ensuring DNS response integrity through cryptographic signature validation.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: No DNSSEC-validating resolver detected on XCP-ng. Install and configure unbound or enable DNSSEC in dnsmasq to request data integrity verification on DNS name/address resolution responses. See V-207489 remediation for detailed steps.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: DNSSEC-validating resolver is installed on XCP-ng and can perform data integrity verification on DNS responses. DNSSEC validates RRSIG signatures against DNSKEY records to ensure DNS response integrity from authoritative sources, preventing DNS spoofing and cache poisoning attacks.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: No DNSSEC-validating resolver installed on XCP-ng. Install unbound (yum install unbound) and configure dnssec-validation to perform data integrity verification on DNS responses. See V-207489 remediation for detailed steps.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: DNSSEC-capable resolver is installed on XCP-ng. Data origin verification authentication is performed through DNSSEC chain of trust from root trust anchor to authoritative zone signatures, authenticating that DNS responses originate from legitimate authoritative sources.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: No DNSSEC-validating resolver detected on XCP-ng. Install and configure unbound or bind with DNSSEC validation to perform data origin verification on DNS responses. See V-207489 remediation for detailed steps.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check verified that DoD PKI certificate authorities are present in the system trust bundle on XCP-ng. The system CA trust store includes DoD Root and Intermediate CAs for verification of protected session establishment, ensuring only DoD PKI-established CAs are used for TLS/SSL certificate validation.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: No DoD PKI certificate authorities detected in the system trust bundle on XCP-ng. To remediate: Download DoD Root CA and Intermediate CA certificates from https://militarycac.com/dodcerts.htm or DISA PKI. Import certificates into /etc/pki/ca-trust/source/anchors/ and run update-ca-trust to update the system trust store. Remove non-DoD CA certificates if operating in a classified environment.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: Disk encryption (LUKS/dm-crypt) is configured on XCP-ng. Cryptographic mechanisms protect information at rest from unauthorized modification by ensuring data integrity through authenticated encryption. LUKS provides sector-level encryption preventing unauthorized modification of stored data including VM disk images, configuration, and hypervisor state.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: No disk encryption detected on XCP-ng. To remediate: Configure LUKS full-disk encryption on all VMM storage volumes. For new installations, enable encryption during install. For existing systems, back up data, create LUKS volumes (cryptsetup luksFormat), restore data. Additionally install AIDE (yum install aide) for file integrity monitoring to detect unauthorized modifications.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: Disk encryption (LUKS) is configured on XCP-ng to prevent unauthorized disclosure of information at rest. Encrypted volumes protect VM disk images, configuration data, and hypervisor state from unauthorized access to physical storage media. LUKS provides AES-256 encryption meeting DoD requirements for data-at-rest protection.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: No disk encryption detected on XCP-ng. Configure LUKS encryption on all VMM storage volumes containing sensitive data including VM disk images and configuration to prevent unauthorized disclosure of information at rest. See V-207494 remediation for detailed LUKS setup steps.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment:
XCP-ng hypervisor maintains separate execution domains for each executing process through multiple kernel-enforced isolation mechanisms.
The automated check verified: (1) Address Space Layout Randomization (ASLR) is enabled via kernel.randomize_va_space sysctl, providing memory layout randomization for each process (2) NX (No-eXecute) bit support is confirmed via CPU flags and dmesg, preventing code execution in data memory regions (3) Linux cgroups are active, providing resource isolation between process groups (4) Linux namespaces (PID, network, mount, user) provide further process-level isolation
Finding: Not a Finding
Justification: The Xen hypervisor architecture provides hardware-enforced separation between Dom0 and guest VMs at the CPU ring level. Within Dom0, the Linux kernel provides standard process isolation through virtual memory, ASLR, NX bit enforcement, cgroups, and namespaces. Each process operates in its own virtual address space with hardware MMU protection. This meets the VMM SRG requirement for separate execution domains per process.
No additional configuration required.

XCP-ng hypervisor process isolation mechanisms are not fully configured.
The automated check found one or more of the following deficiencies: (1) ASLR is not set to full randomization (kernel.randomize_va_space should be 2) (2) NX bit support is not detected in CPU flags or dmesg output (3) cgroups are not active on the system (4) Process namespace isolation is not available
The VMM SRG requires that the hypervisor maintain a separate execution domain for each executing process to prevent unauthorized information flows and process interference.
Remediation: 1. Enable full ASLR: sysctl -w kernel.randomize_va_space=2 and add to /etc/sysctl.conf 2. Verify NX bit support in BIOS/UEFI settings (should be enabled by default on modern hardware) 3. Verify cgroups are mounted: mount | grep cgroup 4. If cgroups are missing, verify kernel configuration supports CONFIG_CGROUPS
Contact the system administrator to ensure all process isolation mechanisms are properly configured per DoD security requirements.

XCP-ng hypervisor maintains separate execution domains for each guest VM through hardware-assisted virtualization.
The automated check verified: (1) Xen hypervisor is running as a Type 1 (bare-metal) hypervisor with hardware-enforced VM isolation (2) Hardware virtualization extensions (Intel VT-x or AMD-V) are active, providing CPU-level isolation between VMs (3) IOMMU (Intel VT-d or AMD-Vi) is available for DMA protection and device isolation (4) Each guest VM runs in its own Xen domain (domU) with dedicated virtual CPU, memory, and device assignments (5) The Xen hypervisor enforces memory page isolation between domains using hardware MMU and EPT/NPT
Finding: Not a Finding
Justification: XCP-ng uses the Xen hypervisor which provides hardware-enforced separation between guest VMs. Each VM runs in its own domain with isolated virtual address space, CPU scheduling, and I/O channels. The hypervisor mediates all inter-domain communication through explicit grant tables and event channels. This architecture inherently meets the VMM SRG requirement for separate execution domains per guest VM.
No additional configuration required - this is an inherent property of the Xen Type 1 hypervisor architecture.

XCP-ng hypervisor VM isolation could not be fully verified.
The automated check found one or more of the following issues: (1) Xen hypervisor version could not be determined or is below minimum supported version (2) Hardware virtualization extensions (VT-x/AMD-V) are not detected or not enabled (3) IOMMU (VT-d/AMD-Vi) is not available for DMA protection
The VMM SRG requires that the hypervisor maintain a separate execution domain for each guest VM to prevent unauthorized access between virtual machines.
Remediation: 1. Verify hardware virtualization is enabled in BIOS/UEFI (Intel VT-x or AMD-V) 2. Enable IOMMU in BIOS/UEFI (Intel VT-d or AMD-Vi) 3. Verify Xen boot parameters include iommu=on: cat /proc/cmdline 4. Update XCP-ng to a supported version if hypervisor version is outdated 5. Verify VMs are properly isolated: xl list should show each VM in its own domain
Contact the system administrator and ISSO to ensure hardware virtualization features are properly enabled.

XCP-ng hypervisor has rate-limiting measures configured to protect against Denial of Service attacks.
The automated check verified one or more of the following protections are in place: (1) iptables rate-limiting rules are configured (e.g., -m limit --limit or -m hashlimit) to throttle incoming connections (2) TCP SYN cookies are enabled (net.ipv4.tcp_syncookies = 1) to protect against SYN flood attacks (3) fail2ban service is installed and active, providing automated IP blocking for brute-force attempts (4) ICMP rate limiting is configured to prevent ICMP flood attacks
Finding: Not a Finding
Justification: The XCP-ng host has network rate-limiting protections configured to mitigate DoS attacks. SYN cookies provide stateless SYN flood protection at the kernel level. Additional iptables rules or fail2ban provide application-layer rate limiting. These measures collectively satisfy the VMM SRG requirement for DoS protection on network interfaces.
No additional configuration required.

XCP-ng hypervisor does not have sufficient rate-limiting measures to protect against Denial of Service attacks.
The automated check found: (1) No iptables rate-limiting rules detected (no -m limit or -m hashlimit rules) (2) TCP SYN cookies may not be enabled (net.ipv4.tcp_syncookies should be 1) (3) fail2ban is not installed or not active (4) No ICMP rate limiting configured
The VMM SRG requires the hypervisor to implement rate-limiting measures on impacted network interfaces to protect against DoS attacks.
Remediation: 1. Enable SYN cookies: sysctl -w net.ipv4.tcp_syncookies=1 and add to /etc/sysctl.conf 2. Install and configure fail2ban: yum install fail2ban; systemctl enable fail2ban 3.
