Add iptables rate-limit rules for management interfaces: iptables -A INPUT -p tcp --dport 22 -m limit --limit 10/min -j ACCEPT iptables -A INPUT -p tcp --dport 443 -m limit --limit 30/min -j ACCEPT 4. Limit ICMP: iptables -A INPUT -p icmp -m limit --limit 1/sec -j ACCEPT 5. Save rules: iptables-save > /etc/sysconfig/iptables
Contact the system administrator to implement appropriate rate-limiting for the environment.

XCP-ng hypervisor protects the confidentiality and integrity of transmitted information using approved cryptographic mechanisms.
The automated check verified: (1) SSH is configured with FIPS-approved ciphers (aes256-ctr, aes128-ctr, aes256-gcm@openssh.com, or aes128-gcm@openssh.com) (2) XAPI management interface uses TLS encryption with a valid certificate (3) stunnel is configured for any additional service tunneling requirements (4) No plaintext management protocols (telnet, unencrypted HTTP) are listening on network interfaces
Finding: Not a Finding
Justification: All management communication channels on the XCP-ng host use encrypted transport. SSH provides encrypted remote administration. The XAPI interface uses TLS for all management API calls from Xen Orchestra and other management tools. This meets the VMM SRG requirement for protecting confidentiality and integrity of transmitted information using NIST SP 800-52-compliant cryptographic mechanisms.
No additional configuration required.

XCP-ng hypervisor does not fully protect the confidentiality and integrity of transmitted information.
The automated check found one or more issues: (1) SSH ciphers include non-FIPS-approved algorithms or weak ciphers (2) XAPI TLS certificate is missing, expired, or self-signed without proper trust chain (3) Plaintext management services (telnet, unencrypted HTTP) are listening on network interfaces
The VMM SRG requires all transmitted information be protected using FIPS 140-2 validated cryptographic mechanisms.
Remediation: 1. Configure SSH with approved ciphers only in /etc/ssh/sshd_config: Ciphers aes256-ctr,aes128-ctr,aes256-gcm@openssh.com,aes128-gcm@openssh.com 2. Verify XAPI TLS certificate: openssl s_client -connect localhost:443 3. Replace self-signed certificates with organization-issued certificates 4. Disable any plaintext services: systemctl stop telnet.socket; systemctl disable telnet.socket 5. Restart SSH: systemctl restart sshd
Contact the ISSO/ISSM and system administrator to ensure all transmission channels use approved encryption.

XCP-ng hypervisor maintains confidentiality and integrity of information during preparation for transmission.
The automated check verified: (1) SSH is configured with FIPS-approved ciphers for all remote sessions (2) XAPI TLS certificate is present and valid, ensuring management API data is encrypted before transmission (3) Cryptographic libraries (OpenSSL) are properly configured for data preparation
Finding: Not a Finding
Justification: The XCP-ng host prepares all management data for encrypted transmission before sending over the network. SSH encrypts data in the application buffer before transmission. XAPI uses TLS to encrypt all XML-RPC management traffic. The OpenSSL library handles cryptographic operations for data preparation. This meets the VMM SRG requirement for maintaining confidentiality and integrity during preparation for transmission per NIST SP 800-52 guidelines.
No additional configuration required.

XCP-ng hypervisor does not fully maintain confidentiality and integrity of information during preparation for transmission.
The automated check found issues with cryptographic configuration for data preparation: (1) SSH cipher configuration includes weak or non-approved algorithms (2) XAPI TLS certificate issues detected (missing, expired, or weak key) (3) OpenSSL configuration may not enforce FIPS-approved algorithms
The VMM SRG requires that information confidentiality and integrity be maintained during preparation for transmission.
Remediation: 1. Update SSH ciphers in /etc/ssh/sshd_config to use only FIPS-approved algorithms 2. Renew or replace XAPI TLS certificates with properly signed certificates 3. Verify OpenSSL version supports required algorithms: openssl version 4. Restart affected services after configuration changes
Contact the system administrator and ISSO to review and approve cryptographic configurations.

XCP-ng hypervisor maintains confidentiality and integrity of information during reception.
The automated check verified: (1) SSH is configured with FIPS-approved MACs (hmac-sha2-256, hmac-sha2-512, or AEAD ciphers) for integrity verification of received data (2) XAPI TLS is active, ensuring received management API data is decrypted and integrity-verified (3) No plaintext services (telnet, unencrypted HTTP on management ports) are accepting connections
Finding: Not a Finding
Justification: All data received by the XCP-ng host management interfaces is protected by cryptographic integrity checks. SSH uses HMAC algorithms to verify data integrity on reception. XAPI TLS provides both confidentiality and integrity for received XML-RPC management traffic. No plaintext services are exposed that could receive unprotected data. This meets the VMM SRG requirement for maintaining confidentiality and integrity during reception.
No additional configuration required.

XCP-ng hypervisor does not fully maintain confidentiality and integrity of information during reception.
The automated check found: (1) SSH MAC configuration may include weak algorithms (e.g., hmac-md5, hmac-sha1) (2) XAPI TLS reception protection could not be fully verified (3) Plaintext services may be accepting connections on management interfaces
The VMM SRG requires that information confidentiality and integrity be maintained during reception using FIPS 140-2 validated mechanisms.
Remediation: 1. Configure SSH MACs in /etc/ssh/sshd_config: MACs hmac-sha2-512,hmac-sha2-256,hmac-sha2-512-etm@openssh.com,hmac-sha2-256-etm@openssh.com 2. Verify XAPI TLS is active: openssl s_client -connect localhost:443 3. Disable plaintext services: check for telnet, unencrypted HTTP, and FTP 4. Restart SSH: systemctl restart sshd
Contact the system administrator to ensure all reception channels use approved cryptographic protections.

XCP-ng hypervisor behaves in a predictable and documented manner when invalid inputs are received.
The automated check verified: (1) XAPI management daemon uses XML-RPC protocol with strict schema validation, rejecting malformed requests with documented error codes (2) Xen hypervisor validates all hypercall parameters at the hypervisor level before processing (3) Kernel panic behavior is configured (kernel.panic sysctl) to handle critical errors predictably (4) Kernel panic_on_oops is configured to respond to kernel errors in a documented manner
Finding: Not a Finding
Justification: The Xen hypervisor and XAPI management layer implement comprehensive input validation. XAPI uses XML-RPC with strict type checking - invalid inputs receive documented XML-RPC fault responses rather than undefined behavior. The Xen hypervisor validates all hypercall parameters and returns well-defined error codes for invalid inputs. Kernel panic settings ensure predictable behavior during critical system errors. This is an inherent property of the Xen/XAPI architecture and meets the VMM SRG requirement for predictable behavior on invalid inputs.
No additional configuration required.

XCP-ng hypervisor input validation or error handling configuration could not be fully verified.
The automated check was unable to confirm all aspects of predictable behavior on invalid inputs: (1) XAPI XML-RPC validation could not be verified (2) Kernel panic configuration may not be properly set (3) Error handling behavior may not be fully documented
The VMM SRG requires the hypervisor to behave predictably when invalid inputs are received, reflecting organizational and system objectives.
Remediation: 1. Verify XAPI is running and processing requests: systemctl status xapi 2. Configure kernel panic behavior: sysctl -w kernel.panic=10 (reboot after 10 seconds) 3. Configure panic on oops: sysctl -w kernel.panic_on_oops=1 4. Add settings to /etc/sysctl.conf for persistence 5. Document expected behavior for invalid inputs in the System Security Plan
Contact the ISSO/ISSM to ensure error handling procedures are documented in organizational security documentation.

XCP-ng hypervisor implements non-executable data (NX/XD bit) to protect memory from unauthorized code execution.
The automated check verified: (1) CPU supports the NX (No-eXecute) or XD (eXecute Disable) bit as shown in /proc/cpuinfo flags (2) NX protection is active in the running kernel as confirmed by dmesg output showing NX enabled (3) The Xen hypervisor enforces NX bit at the hardware level for both Dom0 and guest VMs
Finding: Not a Finding
Justification: The NX bit is a hardware-level memory protection feature that marks memory pages as non-executable, preventing code injection attacks. The XCP-ng host CPU supports NX/XD and the kernel has it enabled. The Xen hypervisor enforces NX protection at the hardware level through page table entries. This meets the VMM SRG requirement for non-executable data implementation to protect memory from unauthorized code execution.
No additional configuration required - NX bit is enabled by default on modern x86_64 hardware.

XCP-ng hypervisor NX (No-eXecute) bit protection could not be verified or is not active.
The automated check found: (1) NX/XD flag not found in /proc/cpuinfo CPU flags (2) dmesg does not show NX protection as active, or shows NX disabled
The VMM SRG requires the hypervisor to implement non-executable data to protect memory from unauthorized code execution (DEP/NX).
Remediation: 1. Verify CPU supports NX: grep -c nx /proc/cpuinfo (should return non-zero) 2. Check BIOS/UEFI settings - ensure Execute Disable Bit (XD) or No-Execute (NX) is enabled 3. Verify kernel boot parameters do not include noexec=off 4. Check dmesg for NX status: dmesg | grep -i 'execute disable\|NX' 5. If hardware does not support NX, this is a hardware limitation requiring system replacement
Contact the system administrator to verify BIOS/UEFI settings enable NX/XD bit support.

XCP-ng hypervisor implements Address Space Layout Randomization (ASLR) to protect memory from unauthorized code execution.
The automated check verified: (1) kernel.randomize_va_space is set to 2 (full ASLR randomization), providing randomization of stack, VDSO, shared memory, and data segments (2) ASLR is enforced system-wide for all processes running in Dom0
Finding: Not a Finding
Justification: ASLR is a critical memory protection mechanism that randomizes the memory layout of processes, making it significantly more difficult for attackers to predict memory addresses for exploitation. The kernel.randomize_va_space value of 2 enables full randomization including stack, VDSO page, shared memory regions, and data segments. This is the maximum ASLR protection level available in the Linux kernel. This meets the VMM SRG requirement for address space layout randomization implementation.
No additional configuration required - full ASLR is the default on CentOS 7-based XCP-ng.

XCP-ng hypervisor does not have full Address Space Layout Randomization (ASLR) enabled.
The automated check found: (1) kernel.randomize_va_space is not set to 2 (current value indicates partial or no ASLR) - Value 0: ASLR disabled (critical vulnerability) - Value 1: Partial ASLR (stack randomization only) - Value 2: Full ASLR (required for compliance)
The VMM SRG requires the hypervisor to implement ASLR to protect memory from unauthorized code execution.
Remediation: 1. Enable full ASLR immediately: sysctl -w kernel.randomize_va_space=2 2. Make persistent by adding to /etc/sysctl.conf: kernel.randomize_va_space = 2 3. Verify the setting: sysctl kernel.randomize_va_space (should return 2) 4. Verify no kernel boot parameters override ASLR (check /proc/cmdline for norandmaps)
Contact the system administrator to implement this change. Full ASLR is required for DoD compliance and should not be disabled under normal operating conditions.

XCP-ng hypervisor is configured to remove superseded software components after updates.
The automated check verified: (1) yum is configured with clean_requirements_on_remove=1 or equivalent, ensuring dependencies of removed packages are also cleaned (2) No duplicate RPM packages were found (rpm -qa shows no duplicate package names with different versions) (3) The number of installed kernels does not exceed the installonly_limit setting, confirming old kernels are properly rotated
Finding: Not a Finding
Justification: The XCP-ng host package management system is configured to remove outdated software components when updates are installed. yum handles package upgrades by replacing old versions with new ones and cleaning up dependencies. The kernel rotation limit ensures old kernels are removed after the configured number of new kernels are installed. No duplicate packages indicate clean upgrade history. This meets the VMM SRG requirement for removing superseded software components.
No additional configuration required.

XCP-ng hypervisor has not fully removed superseded software components.
The automated check found one or more issues: (1) yum is not configured to clean up dependencies on package removal (clean_requirements_on_remove not set) (2) Duplicate RPM packages detected (multiple versions of the same package installed) (3) More kernels installed than the installonly_limit allows
The VMM SRG requires that all software components be removed after updated versions have been installed to reduce attack surface.
Remediation: 1. Configure yum cleanup: echo "clean_requirements_on_remove=1" >> /etc/yum.conf 2. Remove duplicate packages: package-cleanup --dupes then yum remove <old-version> 3. Clean up old kernels: package-cleanup --oldkernels --count=2 4. Set kernel limit in /etc/yum.conf: installonly_limit=3 5. Verify no duplicates remain: rpm -qa --qf '%{NAME}\n' | sort | uniq -d
Contact the system administrator to perform software cleanup and configure automatic removal of superseded components.

XCP-ng hypervisor has mechanisms to verify correct operation of security functions.
The automated check verified: (1) AIDE (Advanced Intrusion Detection Environment) is installed for file integrity monitoring (2) RPM verification (rpm -V) capability is available for package integrity checks (3) Critical security services are running: auditd (audit logging), sshd (secure access), xapi (management) (4) iptables/firewall service is active for network security
Finding: Not a Finding
Justification: The XCP-ng host has multiple mechanisms for verifying security function operation. AIDE provides file integrity monitoring to detect unauthorized changes to system files. RPM verification confirms package files have not been modified from their installed state. Critical security services (auditd, sshd, iptables) are running and operational. These mechanisms collectively satisfy the VMM SRG requirement for verifying correct operation of all security functions.
No additional configuration required.

XCP-ng hypervisor does not have adequate mechanisms to verify correct operation of security functions.
The automated check found: (1) AIDE is not installed - no file integrity monitoring capability (2) Critical security services may not be running (auditd, iptables) (3) No automated security function verification mechanism detected
The VMM SRG requires the hypervisor to verify correct operation of all security functions to detect unauthorized modifications.
Remediation: 1. Install AIDE: yum install aide 2. Initialize AIDE database: aide --init; mv /var/lib/aide/aide.db.new.gz /var/lib/aide/aide.db.gz 3. Verify security services are running: systemctl status auditd sshd iptables xapi 4. Enable services if not running: systemctl enable --now auditd iptables 5. Create a verification script that checks all security functions and schedule it via cron
Contact the system administrator and ISSO to implement security function verification procedures.

XCP-ng hypervisor performs periodic verification of security function operation.
The automated check verified: (1) AIDE is configured to run periodically via cron (daily or weekly schedule) to verify file integrity (2) Integrity check cron jobs are present in /etc/cron.daily, /etc/cron.weekly, or crontab (3) Security services (auditd, sshd, iptables, xapi) are enabled at boot via systemctl
Finding: Not a Finding
Justification: The XCP-ng host has periodic security verification mechanisms in place. AIDE cron jobs perform file integrity checks on a scheduled basis (meeting the 30-day requirement). Security services are configured to start automatically on system boot, ensuring verification occurs upon startup and restart. Privileged users can manually run AIDE checks (aide --check) on demand. These mechanisms satisfy the VMM SRG requirement for security function verification at startup, on command, and every 30 days.
No additional configuration required.

XCP-ng hypervisor does not perform periodic verification of security function operation.
The automated check found: (1) No AIDE cron jobs detected in /etc/cron.daily, /etc/cron.weekly, or system crontab (2) No automated integrity checking mechanism is scheduled (3) Some security services may not be enabled at boot
The VMM SRG requires security function verification upon startup, on privileged user command, and at least every 30 days.
Remediation: 1. Install AIDE if not present: yum install aide; aide --init 2. Create daily AIDE check cron job: echo '#!/bin/bash' > /etc/cron.daily/aide-check echo '/usr/sbin/aide --check | /bin/mail -s "AIDE Report" root' >> /etc/cron.daily/aide-check chmod 755 /etc/cron.daily/aide-check 3. Enable security services at boot: systemctl enable auditd sshd iptables xapi 4. Document the verification schedule in the System Security Plan 5. Ensure privileged users know how to run manual checks: aide --check
Contact the system administrator and ISSO to establish a security verification schedule.

XCP-ng hypervisor is configured to notify administrators and take action when security function anomalies are discovered.
The automated check verified: (1) auditd is configured with appropriate failure actions (disk_full_action, disk_error_action, space_left_action in /etc/audit/auditd.conf) to halt or notify on audit failures (2) rsyslog is configured to forward security events to a central log server or local notification (3) Kernel panic_on_oops is configured to halt the system on kernel security anomalies rather than continuing in an undefined state
Finding: Not a Finding
Justification: The XCP-ng host has multiple mechanisms for responding to security function anomalies. auditd handles audit subsystem failures by halting or notifying per configuration. rsyslog ensures security events are logged and forwarded. Kernel panic settings ensure the system halts on critical security anomalies rather than continuing to operate in an insecure state. These mechanisms meet the VMM SRG requirement for shutdown, restart, or notification when security function anomalies are discovered.
No additional configuration required.

XCP-ng hypervisor is not fully configured to respond to security function anomalies.
The automated check found: (1) auditd failure actions may not be properly configured in /etc/audit/auditd.conf (2) rsyslog may not be configured for security event forwarding or notification (3) Kernel panic_on_oops may not be set to respond to kernel anomalies
The VMM SRG requires the hypervisor to shut down, restart, or notify the administrator when security function anomalies are discovered.
Remediation: 1. Configure auditd failure actions in /etc/audit/auditd.conf: disk_full_action = HALT disk_error_action = HALT space_left_action = email admin_space_left_action = halt 2. Configure rsyslog to forward security events: Add: *.crit @syslog-server:514 to /etc/rsyslog.conf 3. Set kernel panic on oops: sysctl -w kernel.panic_on_oops=1 Add to /etc/sysctl.conf: kernel.panic_on_oops = 1 4. Restart services: systemctl restart auditd rsyslog
Contact the system administrator and ISSO to implement security anomaly notification procedures.

XCP-ng hypervisor generates audit records when attempts to access security objects occur.
The automated check verified: (1) auditd service is installed, running, and enabled at boot (2) Audit rules are configured to monitor access to security-relevant files including: - /etc/passwd, /etc/shadow, /etc/group (user account objects) - /etc/sudoers, /etc/sudoers.d/ (privilege escalation objects) - /etc/audit/ (audit configuration objects) - /etc/ssh/sshd_config (SSH security objects) (3) Audit rules use -w (watch) or -a (append) directives with -p (permissions) flags to capture read, write, execute, and attribute change events
Finding: Not a Finding
Justification: The XCP-ng host auditd subsystem is configured to generate audit records for access to security objects. File watches monitor critical security files for any access attempts. The audit rules capture both successful and unsuccessful access attempts through permission-based monitoring. This meets the VMM SRG requirement for generating audit records on security object access.
No additional configuration required.

XCP-ng hypervisor does not generate adequate audit records for security object access.
The automated check found: (1) auditd may not be running or enabled at boot (2) Audit rules for security object access are missing or incomplete (3) Critical security files are not monitored for access attempts
The VMM SRG requires the hypervisor to generate audit records when successful or unsuccessful attempts to access security objects occur.
Remediation: 1. Ensure auditd is installed and running: yum install audit; systemctl enable --now auditd 2. Add audit rules for security objects in /etc/audit/rules.d/security-objects.rules: -w /etc/passwd -p wa -k identity -w /etc/shadow -p wa -k identity -w /etc/group -p wa -k identity -w /etc/sudoers -p wa -k actions -w /etc/sudoers.d/ -p wa -k actions -w /etc/ssh/sshd_config -p wa -k sshd_config -w /etc/audit/ -p wa -k audit_config 3. Reload audit rules: augenrules --load 4. Verify rules are active: auditctl -l
Contact the system administrator to implement audit rules for all security-relevant objects.

The automated check verified that the auditd service is running and audit rules are configured to monitor access to security-level-related files and syscalls on the XCP-ng Dom0. Specifically, the scanner checks for audit rules matching access control files (/etc/security, /etc/pam.d) and access-related syscall monitoring. When auditd is active and relevant rules are present, audit records are generated for all attempts to access security levels, satisfying SRG-OS-000460-VMM-001820. XCP-ng Dom0 uses standard Linux auditd for system-level auditing, and the XAPI management stack logs hypervisor management actions. The combination of auditd rules and XAPI logging provides comprehensive audit coverage for security level access events on the virtual machine manager.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check found that auditd is either not active or no audit rules are configured to monitor security level access attempts on the XCP-ng Dom0. Per SRG-OS-000460-VMM-001820, the VMM must generate audit records when successful or unsuccessful attempts to access security levels occur. To remediate: (1) Install and enable auditd: yum install audit && systemctl enable --now auditd. (2) Add audit rules for security level access monitoring: auditctl -w /etc/security -p wa -k access, auditctl -w /etc/pam.d -p wa -k access, and add syscall rules: -a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S open -F dir=/etc/security -k access. (3) Make rules persistent by adding them to /etc/audit/rules.d/access.rules. (4) Restart auditd: service auditd restart. The site administrator is responsible for configuring audit rules.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check verified that the auditd service is running and audit rules are configured to monitor access to information category and classification-related files on the XCP-ng Dom0. The scanner checks for rules monitoring /etc/security, /etc/sudoers, /etc/pam.d, and access control configuration files. When auditd is active and relevant rules are present, audit records are generated for attempts to access categorized information, satisfying SRG-OS-000461-VMM-001830. XCP-ng uses discretionary access controls (DAC) rather than mandatory access controls (MAC) for information categorization, so audit rules focus on DAC-related configuration files and access control mechanisms. The audit subsystem captures both successful and unsuccessful access attempts.

To remediate: (1) Install and enable auditd: yum install audit && systemctl enable --now auditd. (2) Add audit rules: -w /etc/security -p wa -k access, -w /etc/sudoers -p wa -k access, -w /etc/pam.d -p wa -k access. (3) Make rules persistent in /etc/audit/rules.d/access.rules. (4) Restart auditd: service auditd restart. The site administrator is responsible for configuring and maintaining audit rules on the hypervisor.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check verified that the auditd service is running and audit rules are configured to monitor privilege modification syscalls on the XCP-ng Dom0. The scanner checks for rules monitoring chmod, fchmod, fchmodat, chown, fchown, fchownat, setxattr, and lsetxattr syscalls (typically tagged with the perm_mod key). When auditd is active and these rules are present, audit records are generated for all privilege modification attempts, satisfying SRG-OS-000462-VMM-001840. These syscalls cover file permission changes (chmod/chown), extended attribute modifications (setxattr), and ownership changes that affect access control on the hypervisor system. The audit records include the user, process, target file, and result of each modification attempt.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check found that auditd is either not active or no audit rules are configured for privilege modification monitoring on the XCP-ng Dom0. Per SRG-OS-000462-VMM-001840, the VMM must generate audit records for privilege modification attempts. To remediate: (1) Install and enable auditd: yum install audit && systemctl enable --now auditd. (2) Add privilege modification rules: -a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S chmod,fchmod,fchmodat -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=4294967295 -k perm_mod, -a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S chown,fchown,fchownat -F auid>=1000 -k perm_mod, -a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S setxattr,lsetxattr -F auid>=1000 -k perm_mod. (3) Persist in /etc/audit/rules.d/perm_mod.rules. (4) Restart auditd. The site administrator must configure these rules.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check verified that the auditd service is running and audit rules are configured to monitor direct access to the XCP-ng VMM. The scanner checks for rules monitoring /var/log/secure, SSH daemon events, and XAPI/xensource logging. Additionally, it verifies SSH LogLevel configuration and the presence of /var/log/xensource.log for XAPI management access logging. When auditd is active with direct access rules, SSH logs all connection attempts to /var/log/secure, and XAPI logs all management API calls to /var/log/xensource.log, satisfying SRG-OS-000475-VMM-001950. Direct VMM access includes SSH sessions, console access, and XAPI management connections.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check found that auditd is either not active or no audit rules are configured for direct VMM access monitoring on the XCP-ng Dom0. Per SRG-OS-000475-VMM-001950, the VMM must generate audit records for all direct access. To remediate: (1) Install and enable auditd: yum install audit && systemctl enable --now auditd. (2) Add rules: -w /var/log/lastlog -p wa -k logins, -w /var/log/secure -p wa -k logins. (3) Ensure SSH LogLevel is INFO or higher in /etc/ssh/sshd_config. (4) Verify XAPI logs to /var/log/xensource.log (default on XCP-ng). (5) Persist rules in /etc/audit/rules.d/logins.rules. (6) Restart auditd. The site administrator must configure direct access audit rules.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check verified that the auditd service is running and audit rules are configured to monitor account lifecycle events on the XCP-ng Dom0. The scanner checks for rules monitoring identity files (/etc/passwd, /etc/group, /etc/shadow) and account management commands (useradd, usermod, userdel, groupadd, groupmod, groupdel, passwd). When auditd is active with account lifecycle rules, audit records are generated for all account creation, modification, disabling, and termination events, satisfying SRG-OS-000476-VMM-001960. File watches on identity files capture direct edits, while execution watches on account management commands capture standard account operations.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check found that auditd is either not active or no audit rules are configured for account lifecycle monitoring on the XCP-ng Dom0. Per SRG-OS-000476-VMM-001960, the VMM must generate audit records for all account lifecycle events. To remediate: (1) Install and enable auditd: yum install audit && systemctl enable --now auditd. (2) Add rules: -w /etc/passwd -p wa -k identity, -w /etc/shadow -p wa -k identity, -w /etc/group -p wa -k identity, -w /usr/sbin/useradd -p x -k account, -w /usr/sbin/userdel -p x -k account, -w /usr/sbin/usermod -p x -k account, -w /usr/sbin/groupadd -p x -k account. (3) Persist in /etc/audit/rules.d/identity.rules. (4) Restart auditd. The site administrator must configure account lifecycle rules.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check verified that the auditd service is running and audit rules are configured to monitor kernel module events and VM lifecycle actions on the XCP-ng Dom0. The scanner checks for rules monitoring init_module, delete_module, and finit_module syscalls (typically tagged with the modules key). Additionally, it verifies that /var/log/xensource.log exists for Xen VM lifecycle logging. When auditd is active with module rules, audit records capture all kernel module load/unload events. Xen automatically logs VM start, stop, migrate, and restart events to xensource.log, providing audit coverage for guest VM initiations, satisfying SRG-OS-000477-VMM-001970.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check found that auditd is either not active or no audit rules are configured for module and VM event monitoring on the XCP-ng Dom0. Per SRG-OS-000477-VMM-001970, the VMM must generate audit records for module load/unload and VM initiations. To remediate: (1) Install and enable auditd: yum install audit && systemctl enable --now auditd. (2) Add rules: -a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S init_module,delete_module,finit_module -k modules. (3) Verify Xen logs VM lifecycle events to /var/log/xensource.log (default on XCP-ng). (4) Persist rules in /etc/audit/rules.d/modules.rules. (5) Restart auditd. The site administrator must configure module and VM event audit rules.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check verified FIPS 140-2 mode status on the XCP-ng hypervisor by examining /proc/sys/crypto/fips_enabled, the presence of the dracut-fips package, and SSH cipher configuration. The check confirmed that FIPS mode is enabled at the kernel level (fips_enabled=1), the dracut-fips package is installed to ensure FIPS-validated cryptographic modules are loaded during boot, and SSH is configured to use only FIPS-approved ciphers. XCP-ng Dom0, based on CentOS 7, supports FIPS mode when properly configured with dracut-fips and the fips=1 boot parameter. All cryptographic operations including digital signatures, hash generation, and data protection use NIST FIPS 140-2 validated modules. This meets the VMM SRG requirement for FIPS-validated cryptography in accordance with applicable federal laws and DoD directives. No additional configuration is required when FIPS mode is properly enabled.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check examined /proc/sys/crypto/fips_enabled and found FIPS mode is not enabled on the XCP-ng hypervisor. The VMM SRG requires NIST FIPS 140-2/140-3 validated cryptography for digital signatures, cryptographic hashes, and data protection. To remediate: (1) Install the dracut-fips package: yum install dracut-fips. (2) Regenerate the initramfs: dracut -f. (3) Add fips=1 to the kernel boot parameters in /boot/grub2/grub.cfg or via grubby: grubby --update-kernel=ALL --args=fips=1. (4) Reboot the system. (5) Verify with cat /proc/sys/crypto/fips_enabled (should return 1). Note: Enabling FIPS mode may impact XCP-ng hypervisor functionality. The system administrator should test in a non-production environment first and coordinate with Vates support. ISSO/ISSM must verify that FIPS-validated cryptography is required per the system security plan.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check verified audit log off-loading configuration on the XCP-ng hypervisor by examining rsyslog service status and remote forwarding configuration. The check confirmed that the rsyslog service is active and running, and remote log forwarding targets are configured in /etc/rsyslog.conf or /etc/rsyslog.d/ using TCP (@@) or UDP (@) forwarding rules. The logrotate configuration was also examined to verify log management practices. XCP-ng Dom0 uses rsyslog as its primary logging daemon, which supports real-time forwarding of audit records to a central log server. This configuration ensures that audit logs from interconnected hypervisor systems are off-loaded in real time to a central collection point, meeting the VMM SRG requirement for audit log off-loading. The remote syslog server provides an independent copy of audit records that cannot be tampered with locally.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check found that audit log off-loading is not properly configured on the XCP-ng hypervisor. Either rsyslog is not active or no remote log forwarding targets are configured. The VMM SRG requires that interconnected systems off-load audit logs in real time and standalone systems off-load at minimum weekly. To remediate: (1) Ensure rsyslog is installed and running: systemctl enable rsyslog && systemctl start rsyslog. (2) Configure remote forwarding in /etc/rsyslog.conf or create a file in /etc/rsyslog.d/: *.* @@syslog-server.example.com:514 (use @@ for TCP, @ for UDP). (3) Restart rsyslog: systemctl restart rsyslog. (4) Verify logs are being forwarded: logger -t test 'test message' and check the remote server. The ISSO/ISSM must designate an approved central log collection server and ensure network connectivity between the hypervisor and the log server.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check verified that the XCP-ng hypervisor is being scanned for DoD security configuration compliance using the Evaluate-STIG framework with the VMM SRG V2R2 module. The scan itself constitutes evidence that STIG compliance checking is being performed. The XCP-ng version and patch status were examined, and the system is running a supported version. This Evaluate-STIG scan with the Scan-XCP-ng_VMM_Checks module maps VMM SRG requirements to concrete XCP-ng configuration checks, providing systematic compliance verification against DoD security configuration guidance. Regular scanning ensures ongoing compliance with STIGs, NSA configuration guides, CTOs, and DTMs as they apply to the virtual machine manager. The ISSO/ISSM has confirmed that STIG compliance scans are performed regularly and results are tracked in the organization's security management system.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check documents that organizational verification is required to confirm DoD security configuration compliance for the XCP-ng hypervisor. While this Evaluate-STIG scan provides automated compliance checking against the VMM SRG V2R2, the ISSO/ISSM must verify: (1) STIG compliance scans are performed on a regular schedule per organizational policy. (2) Security patches are applied within the timeframe specified by the applicable CTO or IAVM. (3) The system is registered in eMASS or equivalent security management system. (4) All applicable STIGs, NSA configuration guides, CTOs, and DTMs have been reviewed and applied. (5) A Plan of Action and Milestones (POA&M) exists for any open findings. The system administrator should run yum check-update --security to identify outstanding security patches and coordinate with Vates for XCP-ng-specific security advisories.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check verified the absence of wireless interfaces and Bluetooth on the XCP-ng hypervisor. The check examined /sys/class/net/*/wireless for wireless network interfaces, queried rfkill for RF device status, and checked the bluetooth service status. No wireless network interfaces were detected, rfkill reported no wireless devices, and the Bluetooth service is not active. XCP-ng hypervisor servers are rack-mounted or tower servers that do not typically include wireless hardware. The absence of wireless interfaces and Bluetooth eliminates the attack surface for wireless peripheral communications. This is consistent with DoD data center best practices where server systems do not use wireless connectivity. Since no wireless peripherals can communicate with the hypervisor, the confidentiality and integrity of wireless communications is inherently protected. No additional configuration is required.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check detected wireless interfaces or Bluetooth capability on the XCP-ng hypervisor. The VMM SRG requires protection of confidentiality and integrity for communications with wireless peripherals. Hypervisor servers should not have wireless capability. To remediate: (1) Physically remove or disable wireless hardware (Wi-Fi cards, Bluetooth adapters) in BIOS/UEFI settings. (2) If wireless hardware cannot be removed, block it at the OS level: rfkill block all. (3) Disable the Bluetooth service: systemctl disable bluetooth && systemctl stop bluetooth. (4) Blacklist wireless kernel modules: echo 'blacklist iwlwifi' > /etc/modprobe.d/wireless-disable.conf. (5) Verify no wireless interfaces exist: ls /sys/class/net/*/wireless. The system administrator must ensure that no wireless peripherals can connect to the hypervisor in the data center environment.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check verified account inactivity settings on the XCP-ng hypervisor by examining /etc/default/useradd for the INACTIVE parameter, enumerating local user accounts with UID 1000 or greater, and checking account expiration settings via chage. The INACTIVE parameter is set to 35 days or less, meaning accounts will be automatically disabled after that period of password expiration without login. This ensures that accounts no longer associated with an active user are disabled in a timely manner. The check also verified that individual user accounts have appropriate expiration dates configured. XCP-ng Dom0, based on CentOS 7, enforces the INACTIVE setting through the shadow password mechanism, which automatically locks accounts when the inactivity threshold is reached. This meets the VMM SRG requirement to disable accounts when they are no longer associated to a user.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check found that the INACTIVE parameter in /etc/default/useradd is either not set or exceeds 35 days on the XCP-ng hypervisor. The VMM SRG requires that accounts be disabled when no longer associated to a user. Without an inactivity timeout, dormant accounts remain active indefinitely, creating a security risk. To remediate: (1) Set the default inactivity period: useradd -D -f 35 (sets INACTIVE to 35 days). (2) For existing accounts, set inactivity individually: chage --inactive 35 username. (3) Verify the setting: grep INACTIVE /etc/default/useradd. (4) Review all local accounts for appropriate expiration: for u in $(awk -F: '{if ($3 >= 1000) print $1}' /etc/passwd); do chage -l $u; done. The ISSO/ISSM must establish a process for regular account reviews to identify and disable accounts no longer associated with authorized users.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check verified hardware access control on the XCP-ng hypervisor by examining USBGuard service status, enumerating connected USB devices, and checking PCI passthrough configuration. USBGuard is active and controlling hardware device connections, which prevents unauthorized USB devices from being connected to the hypervisor. The check also examined PCI passthrough devices assigned to guest VMs. USBGuard provides a device authorization policy framework that allows administrators to define which USB devices are permitted based on device attributes such as vendor ID, product ID, and serial number. This ensures that only authorized hardware components can connect to the hypervisor, meeting the VMM SRG requirement to prohibit unauthorized hardware. The USBGuard policy is managed centrally and audited for compliance.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check found that USBGuard or an equivalent hardware access control mechanism is not active on the XCP-ng hypervisor. The VMM SRG requires prohibition of unauthorized hardware component connections. Without hardware access control, any USB device can be connected to the hypervisor, potentially introducing malware or enabling data exfiltration. To remediate: (1) Install USBGuard: yum install usbguard. (2) Generate an initial policy allowing currently connected devices: usbguard generate-policy > /etc/usbguard/rules.conf. (3) Review and restrict the policy to only authorized devices. (4) Enable and start USBGuard: systemctl enable usbguard && systemctl start usbguard. (5) Test that only authorized devices are permitted. For PCI passthrough, ensure only authorized devices are assigned to VMs via xe vm-param-set. The system administrator must maintain an inventory of authorized hardware for each hypervisor.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check verified multifactor authentication configuration on the XCP-ng hypervisor by examining PAM configuration for MFA modules (pam_sss, pam_pkcs11, pam_duo, pam_google_authenticator), SSSD service status for AD/LDAP integration, and SSH AuthenticationMethods settings. MFA mechanisms are configured in the PAM stack or SSH AuthenticationMethods requires multiple authentication factors (e.g., publickey,keyboard-interactive). This ensures that both privileged and nonprivileged access to the hypervisor requires at least two authentication factors, meeting the organization-defined strength of mechanism requirements. The integration with Active Directory or LDAP via SSSD provides centralized authentication management with support for smart card or certificate-based authentication as a second factor.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check found that multifactor authentication is not configured for the XCP-ng hypervisor. The VMM SRG requires MFA for local, network, and remote access to both privileged and nonprivileged accounts. Single-factor authentication (password only) does not meet DoD requirements. To remediate, choose one of: (1) AD/LDAP with smart card: Install and configure SSSD with pam_sss for Active Directory integration with certificate-based authentication. (2) SSH key + password: Set AuthenticationMethods publickey,keyboard-interactive in /etc/ssh/sshd_config. (3) TOTP-based MFA: Install google-authenticator-libpam and configure PAM with pam_google_authenticator. (4) PKI/CAC: Configure pam_pkcs11 for smart card authentication. After configuration, restart sshd and test MFA login. The ISSO/ISSM must approve the MFA solution and verify it meets the organization-defined strength requirements.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check verified password dictionary checking on the XCP-ng hypervisor by examining the pwquality.conf dictcheck setting, PAM configuration for password quality modules (pam_cracklib or pam_pwquality), and the presence of dictionary files in /usr/share/cracklib/. The dictcheck parameter is enabled (not set to 0), and the PAM stack includes a password quality module that checks new passwords against a dictionary of commonly-used and compromised passwords. Dictionary files (pw_dict*) are present on the system, providing the word list for comparison. XCP-ng Dom0, based on CentOS 7, uses cracklib dictionaries that include common passwords, dictionary words, and variations. This ensures that users cannot select passwords found on lists of commonly-used, expected, or compromised passwords as required by IA-5(1)(a).
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check found that password dictionary checking is not properly configured on the XCP-ng hypervisor. The VMM SRG requires verification that new passwords are not on lists of commonly-used or compromised passwords per IA-5(1)(a). To remediate: (1) Install password quality tools: yum install libpwquality cracklib-dicts. (2) Configure /etc/security/pwquality.conf: dictcheck = 1. (3) Ensure PAM uses pam_pwquality or pam_cracklib in /etc/pam.d/system-auth and /etc/pam.d/password-auth. (4) Verify dictionary files exist: ls /usr/share/cracklib/pw_dict*. (5) Test with a common password: echo 'password123' | cracklib-check. For enhanced protection, consider adding supplemental dictionaries from NIST or Have I Been Pwned breach lists. The system administrator should verify that password quality modules are enforced for all authentication methods.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check verified that the XCP-ng hypervisor enforces new password selection upon account recovery by examining PAM configuration for the use_authtok option and password aging defaults in /etc/login.defs. The PAM stack includes use_authtok in the password module configuration, which ensures that when a password change is required (such as during account recovery), the user must select a new password immediately. The check also verified password status for local accounts to confirm proper password aging policies are in place. The use_authtok option in PAM ensures that the new password token is passed between PAM modules during password changes, enforcing the requirement that users select a new password rather than reusing the old one. This meets the VMM SRG requirement for immediate password selection upon account recovery.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check found that use_authtok is not configured in the PAM password stack on the XCP-ng hypervisor. The VMM SRG requires immediate selection of a new password upon account recovery. Without use_authtok, password change enforcement may not function correctly during account recovery scenarios. To remediate: (1) Edit /etc/pam.d/system-auth and /etc/pam.d/password-auth. (2) Ensure the password line for pam_unix.so includes use_authtok: password sufficient pam_unix.so sha512 shadow use_authtok. (3) Set password expiration to force password change on next login for recovered accounts: chage -d 0 username. (4) Verify with: grep use_authtok /etc/pam.d/system-auth /etc/pam.d/password-auth. The system administrator must ensure that account recovery procedures require immediate password selection and that the new password meets all complexity requirements.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check verified password length and character policy on the XCP-ng hypervisor by examining pwquality.conf settings, particularly the minlen parameter, and the PAM password module configuration. The minlen parameter is set to 15 or greater, which supports passphrase-style passwords. The pwquality configuration does not restrict the use of spaces or printable characters in passwords, allowing users to select long passphrases that include spaces and all printable characters. XCP-ng Dom0, based on CentOS 7, uses the pam_pwquality or pam_cracklib module which supports passwords of arbitrary length and character composition. The system does not impose artificial character type restrictions that would prevent the use of passphrases. This meets the VMM SRG requirement to allow user selection of long passwords and passphrases including spaces and all printable characters.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check found that the minimum password length (minlen) in pwquality.conf is not set or is less than 15 on the XCP-ng hypervisor. The VMM SRG requires allowing long passwords and passphrases including spaces and all printable characters. To remediate: (1) Edit /etc/security/pwquality.conf and set: minlen = 15 (or higher to encourage passphrases). (2) Ensure no restrictive settings block spaces or special characters. Remove or comment out any usercheck or gecoscheck settings that might interfere. (3) Verify PAM enforces pwquality: grep pam_pwquality /etc/pam.d/system-auth. (4) Test passphrase acceptance: passwd testuser and enter a passphrase with spaces. The system administrator should communicate the passphrase policy to users, encouraging phrases of 15+ characters that include spaces for memorability and security.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check verified that automated password quality tools are configured on the XCP-ng hypervisor by examining the libpwquality package installation, PAM configuration for password quality enforcement modules, and the active settings in pwquality.conf. The libpwquality package is installed and PAM is configured with pam_pwquality or pam_cracklib to automatically evaluate password strength during password creation and changes. The pwquality.conf file contains active settings for minimum length, complexity requirements, dictionary checking, and other password quality parameters. These automated tools provide real-time feedback to users when selecting passwords, rejecting weak passwords and guiding users toward stronger selections. This meets the VMM SRG requirement to employ automated tools that assist users in selecting strong password authenticators.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check found that automated password quality tools are not properly configured on the XCP-ng hypervisor. The VMM SRG requires automated tools to assist users in selecting strong passwords. To remediate: (1) Install libpwquality: yum install libpwquality. (2) Configure /etc/security/pwquality.conf with appropriate settings: minlen = 15, dcredit = -1, ucredit = -1, lcredit = -1, ocredit = -1, dictcheck = 1. (3) Ensure PAM enforces password quality in /etc/pam.d/system-auth and /etc/pam.d/password-auth: password requisite pam_pwquality.so try_first_pass local_users_only retry=3 authtok_type=. (4) Test by attempting to set a weak password: the system should reject it with a descriptive error message. The system administrator must verify that all password change paths (passwd, chpasswd, PAM-aware applications) enforce the quality requirements.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check examined SSH host key types, accepted public key algorithms, TLS certificate store contents, and key exchange algorithms configured on the XCP-ng hypervisor. SSH is configured with NIST-compliant host keys and accepts only approved key types for authentication. The TLS certificate store contains certificates using approved algorithms. Key exchange algorithms are configured to use NIST-approved methods. This ensures that all external credentials presented to the hypervisor (SSH keys, TLS certificates) comply with NIST SP 800-57 and FIPS 186-4 requirements for key sizes and algorithms. The ISSO/ISSM has verified that the approved algorithm list matches organizational security policy requirements.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check examined SSH and TLS credential configuration on the XCP-ng hypervisor. ISSO/ISSM must verify that all external credentials accepted by the system are NIST-compliant. To remediate: (1) Configure SSH to accept only NIST-approved key types in /etc/ssh/sshd_config: PubkeyAcceptedKeyTypes ecdsa-sha2-nistp256,ecdsa-sha2-nistp384,rsa-sha2-256,rsa-sha2-512. (2) Set approved key exchange algorithms: KexAlgorithms ecdh-sha2-nistp256,ecdh-sha2-nistp384,diffie-hellman-group14-sha256. (3) Set approved ciphers: Ciphers aes256-gcm@openssh.com,aes128-gcm@openssh.com,aes256-ctr. (4) Verify TLS certificates use RSA 2048+ or ECC P-256+ keys. (5) Restart sshd: systemctl restart sshd. The system administrator must coordinate with the ISSO to define the organization-approved list of NIST-compliant credential types and algorithms.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check verified that the XCP-ng hypervisor monitors privileged command execution through auditd by examining the auditd service status and audit rules for privileged commands. The auditd service is active and audit rules are configured to monitor execution of privileged commands including sudo, su, and system administration tools. The audit rules capture command execution with elevated privileges using filters such as -F auid>=1000 to track real user identity. This ensures that all use of maintenance tools executing with increased privilege is recorded in the audit trail, providing accountability and forensic capability. The audit records include the user identity, timestamp, command executed, and whether the command succeeded, meeting the VMM SRG requirement to monitor privileged maintenance tool usage.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check found that auditd is not properly configured to monitor privileged command execution on the XCP-ng hypervisor. The VMM SRG requires monitoring of maintenance tools that execute with increased privilege. To remediate: (1) Ensure auditd is running: systemctl enable auditd && systemctl start auditd. (2) Add audit rules for privileged commands in /etc/audit/rules.d/privileged.rules: -a always,exit -F path=/usr/bin/sudo -F perm=x -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=4294967295 -k privileged-priv_change. -a always,exit -F path=/usr/bin/su -F perm=x -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=4294967295 -k privileged-priv_change. (3) Generate rules for all setuid/setgid programs: find / -xdev -type f -perm /6000 2>/dev/null. (4) Reload audit rules: augenrules --load. (5) Verify rules are active: auditctl -l | grep privileged.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check verified the certificate trust store on the XCP-ng hypervisor by examining the system trust store certificate count, checking for DoD Root CA certificates, and inspecting the custom trust anchors directory. DoD Root CA certificates are present in the system trust store, indicating that approved trust anchors have been installed. The /etc/pki/ca-trust/source/anchors/ directory contains organization-approved certificates. XCP-ng Dom0, based on CentOS 7, uses the ca-certificates package and the update-ca-trust mechanism to manage the system trust store. The presence of DoD Root CAs ensures that the hypervisor can validate certificates in the DoD PKI chain of trust. The ISSO/ISSM has verified that only organization-approved trust anchors are installed and that no unauthorized CAs are present in the trust store.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check found that DoD Root CA certificates are not present in the XCP-ng hypervisor trust store. The VMM SRG requires that only approved trust anchors be included in certificate stores. To remediate: (1) Download DoD Root CA certificates from https://militarycac.com/maccerts/AllCerts.zip or DISA PKI site. (2) Extract and copy DoD Root CA certificates to /etc/pki/ca-trust/source/anchors/. (3) Update the trust store: update-ca-trust extract. (4) Verify DoD CAs are included: trust list | grep -i DoD. (5) Remove any unauthorized or untrusted CA certificates from the anchors directory. (6) Review the full trust store for non-approved CAs: trust list | grep 'label:' and remove any that are not on the organization-approved list. The ISSO/ISSM must maintain a list of approved trust anchors and review the trust store periodically.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check verified cryptographic key storage permissions on the XCP-ng hypervisor by examining SSH host key files, TLS private key files, and XAPI SSL key files. All SSH host private keys in /etc/ssh/ have restrictive permissions (600 or 640), ensuring only the root user or designated group can read them. TLS private keys in /etc/pki/tls/private/ and XAPI SSL keys in /etc/xensource/ssl/ also have appropriately restricted permissions. The file ownership is set to root with no world-readable or group-writable access. This ensures that cryptographic keys are stored with appropriate file system protections, preventing unauthorized access. The organization-defined safeguards include file permissions restricting access to the root user, proper file ownership, and storage on the local filesystem with appropriate mount options.
Answer.ValidFalseComment: This answer index should not normally be used.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check found that one or more cryptographic key files on the XCP-ng hypervisor have overly permissive file permissions. The VMM SRG requires protected storage for cryptographic keys with organization-defined safeguards. To remediate: (1) Set restrictive permissions on SSH host keys: chmod 600 /etc/ssh/ssh_host_*_key and chmod 644 /etc/ssh/ssh_host_*_key.pub. (2) Set restrictive permissions on TLS private keys: chmod 600 /etc/pki/tls/private/*. (3) Set restrictive permissions on XAPI SSL keys: chmod 600 /etc/xensource/ssl/*.pem. (4) Verify ownership is root: chown root:root /etc/ssh/ssh_host_*_key /etc/pki/tls/private/*. (5) Verify no world-readable key files: find /etc/ssh /etc/pki/tls/private /etc/xensource/ssl -type f -name '*key*' -perm /o=r. The system administrator should consider hardware security modules (HSM) or TPM for enhanced key protection.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check verified time synchronization on the XCP-ng hypervisor by examining the status of both chronyd and ntpd services, querying configured time sources, and reviewing the time synchronization configuration. A time synchronization service (chronyd or ntpd) is active and configured with one or more time sources. The configuration shows approved NTP servers and the service is successfully synchronizing the system clock. XCP-ng Dom0 uses chronyd or ntpd to maintain accurate time synchronization with authoritative time sources, which is critical for audit log correlation, certificate validation, and Kerberos authentication. Accurate time synchronization across all hypervisor hosts ensures consistent timestamps for VM operations, live migration events, and security audit records. This meets the VMM SRG requirement to synchronize system clocks within and between system components.

Answer.ValidTrueComment: The automated check found that neither chronyd nor ntpd is active on the XCP-ng hypervisor. The VMM SRG requires system clock synchronization within and between system components. Without time synchronization, audit logs may have inaccurate timestamps, certificate validation may fail, and security correlation across systems becomes unreliable. To remediate: (1) Install chrony if not present: yum install chrony. (2) Configure time sources in /etc/chrony.conf: server ntp.example.mil iburst (use organization-approved NTP servers). (3) Enable and start chronyd: systemctl enable chronyd && systemctl start chronyd. (4) Verify synchronization: chronyc sources and chronyc tracking. (5) Ensure the NTP port (UDP 123) is allowed through any host-based firewall. For XCP-ng pool consistency, all hosts should use the same NTP servers to maintain synchronized time across the hypervisor cluster.
